NordGrid stores a golden_schema.json for every Silver and Gold table in the same repository as the pipeline code. Module 6 (Testing) covers the full contract testing pattern.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Layer all three strategies: schema-on-read validation catches source changes at ingestion time, schema-on-write enforcement catches transformation errors at write time, and contract tests catch code changes at development time. NordGrid's pipeline runs all three: validate the Bronze input schema, enforce the Silver output schema, and the CI suite validates both against golden schema files. Each layer catches a different category of schema problem, and together they form a comprehensive defense.

Lessons 1–3 addressed schema evolution in plain Parquet — the format NordGrid's Silver layer has used since the beginning. Parquet is excellent for read performance but lacks features that production data platforms need: atomic writes (a failed write can leave partial data), update and delete operations (Parquet is append-only), schema enforcement at the storage level (not just in pipeline code), and versioning (no way to read yesterday's data after today's write). Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that adds all of these capabilities on top of Parquet. It stores data as Parquet files but adds a transaction log that tracks every change, enabling ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, and efficient upsert operations. This lesson introduces Delta Lake's architecture and explains why NordGrid is migrating its Silver layer from plain Parquet to Delta.
The Transaction Log

Delta Lake's core innovation is the _delta_log directory — a JSON-based transaction log that records every write, update, delete, and schema change as a sequence of versioned entries. Each entry lists the files added and removed by the transaction. When you read a Delta table, Spark reads the transaction log first to determine which files constitute the current version of the table. When you write, Delta appends a new log entry atomically. If two writers attempt concurrent writes, Delta's optimistic concurrency control detects the conflict and fails the later writer with a ConcurrentModificationException rather than silently producing an inconsistent table. This is the ACID guarantee that plain Parquet cannot provide.

blocks.title: Delta Lake architecture: Parquet files + transaction log
blocks.content:
delta_table/
├── _delta_log/
│   ├── 00000000000000000000.json   ← version 0: initial write (added 4 files)
│   ├── 00000000000000000001.json   ← version 1: append (added 2 files)
│   ├── 00000000000000000002.json   ← version 2: update (added 1 file, removed 1)
│   └── 00000000000000000003.json   ← version 3: schema change (added 'firmware' column)
├── part-00000-...snappy.parquet  ← data files (standard Parquet format)
├── part-00001-...snappy.parquet
├── part-00002-...snappy.parquet
└── part-00003-...snappy.parquet

Reading version 3: log says files 0,1,3 are current (file 2 was replaced in version 2)
Reading version 1: log says files 0,1,2 are current (before the update)
blocks.caption: Delta Lake stores data as standard Parquet files. The _delta_log tracks which files belong to each version. Time travel reads previous versions by consulting the log history.

blocks.content: What Delta Lake Adds to Parquet

rows: No — a failed write can leave partial files
Yes — writes are all-or-nothing via the transaction log
Unsafe — two writers can overwrite each other
Not supported — append or overwrite only
Supported via MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE operations
Enforced on write — rejects data that does not match the table schema
Built-in with mergeSchema and overwriteSchema modes
Read any previous version by number or timestamp
OPTIMIZE command with automatic file management
Full history of every change in the transaction log

blocks.content: Why NordGrid Is Migrating

NordGrid's decision to migrate the Silver layer to Delta Lake was driven by three production incidents. First, a pipeline failure during a Silver write left 3 of 12 region partitions with partial data — the pipeline crashed after writing NORTH, SOUTH, and EAST but before writing the remaining 9 regions. The partial write corrupted the Silver table for 18 hours until the on-call engineer manually deleted the incomplete partitions and re-ran the pipeline. Delta's atomic writes would have prevented this: the failed transaction would have been rolled back, leaving the previous version intact. Second, the meter metadata table required daily updates (customer_type changes), but Parquet's append-only model meant the team had to overwrite the entire table every day, even though only 0.1% of rows changed. Delta's MERGE operation handles this efficiently. Third, the analytics team needed to query yesterday's Silver data after today's pipeline had overwritten it — Delta's time travel makes this trivial.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: You do not need to migrate all tables to Delta immediately. Start with tables that have the most acute pain: tables with concurrent writers, tables that need update/delete, tables where partial write failures have occurred, or tables where time travel would have prevented a data loss incident. NordGrid is migrating Silver first (the most write-heavy layer), then Gold (for MERGE support), and keeping Bronze as plain Parquet (raw files that are append-only by design and do not need Delta's features).

Delta Lake integrates seamlessly with the DataFrame API you already know. Reading a Delta table uses spark.read.format('delta') instead of spark.read.parquet(). Writing uses df.write.format('delta') instead of df.write.parquet(). The API surface is nearly identical to Parquet, with the critical addition that Delta enforces the table's schema on write, tracks every write in the transaction log, and supports ACID transactions. This lesson covers the read and write APIs, the DeltaTable class for table-level operations, and the NordGrid pattern for converting existing Parquet tables to Delta.
Reading and Writing

# Writing a Delta table (creates the table if it does not exist)
df = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings_parquet/')  # existing Parquet data

df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('silver/readings_delta/')

# Reading a Delta table
df_delta = spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings_delta/')
df_delta.printSchema()
df_delta.show(3)

# Appending to an existing Delta table
df_today = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/2026-03-22/', header=True, schema=bronze_schema)
(df_today
    .write
    .format('delta')
    .mode('append')
    .save('silver/readings_delta/')
)
Schema Enforcement on Write

When you append to an existing Delta table, Delta compares the incoming DataFrame's schema against the table's current schema. If the schemas do not match — a column is missing, an extra column is present, or a type differs — Delta rejects the write with an AnalysisException. This is schema enforcement at the storage level, independent of any validation in your pipeline code. It is the ultimate safety net: even if your pipeline's validate_schema() function has a bug, Delta prevents mismatched data from entering the table. NordGrid relies on this enforcement as the last line of defense, in addition to the pipeline-level validation from lesson 3.
The DeltaTable Class

The DeltaTable class provides table-level operations that the DataFrame API does not support: MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE, history, and vacuum. You create a DeltaTable object by pointing it at a Delta directory. From there, you can inspect the table's history (every transaction), execute SQL-like operations (update rows, delete rows, merge new data), and perform maintenance tasks. NordGrid's pipelines use the DeltaTable class for the meter metadata table, which requires daily upserts that the DataFrame API's append and overwrite modes cannot express.

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

# Create a DeltaTable reference
dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/readings_delta/')

# Inspect table history
dt.history().select('version', 'timestamp', 'operation', 'operationMetrics').show(truncate=False)

# Convert an existing Parquet directory to Delta (in-place, no data copy)
DeltaTable.convertToDelta(spark, "parquet.`silver/readings_parquet/`")

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Convert existing Parquet tables to Delta using DeltaTable.convertToDelta() — it adds the _delta_log directory without copying or rewriting any data files. The conversion is instant (a few seconds) regardless of table size. NordGrid converted the 3.2TB Silver table in 4 seconds. After conversion, all reads and writes automatically use Delta's transaction log, schema enforcement, and time travel. The existing Parquet files are untouched.

The MERGE operation is Delta Lake's most powerful feature for production pipelines. It combines insert, update, and delete into a single atomic operation: rows in the source that match the target are updated, rows that do not match are inserted, and optionally, target rows without a source match are deleted. This is the upsert pattern that Parquet cannot express — with Parquet, you must overwrite the entire table or append duplicates. MERGE processes only the changed rows, making it dramatically more efficient for tables where a small fraction of rows change per run. NordGrid's meter metadata table has 1.2 million rows, but only 500–2,000 rows change daily (new meters installed, customer types updated). MERGE processes those 2,000 rows without touching the other 1,198,000.
MERGE Syntax

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_timestamp

# Target: existing meter metadata in Delta
target = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/meters_metadata/')

# Source: today's metadata update from the CRM system
source = spark.read.csv('bronze/crm_export/2026-03-22/', header=True, schema=crm_schema)

# MERGE: upsert — update existing meters, insert new meters
(target.alias('t')
    .merge(
        source.alias('s'),
        't.meter_id = s.meter_id'  # match condition
    )
    .whenMatchedUpdate(set={
        'customer_type': col('s.customer_type'),
        'substation_id': col('s.substation_id'),
        'updated_at': current_timestamp(),
    })
    .whenNotMatchedInsert(values={
        'meter_id': col('s.meter_id'),
        'customer_type': col('s.customer_type'),
        'substation_id': col('s.substation_id'),
        'install_date': col('s.install_date'),
        'updated_at': current_timestamp(),
    })
    .execute()
)
MERGE with Delete

MERGE supports a third clause — whenNotMatchedBySourceDelete() — that deletes target rows with no corresponding source row. NordGrid uses this for the active meter fleet: if a meter was decommissioned and does not appear in today's CRM export, the MERGE deletes it from the metadata table. The three-way MERGE (update matched, insert new, delete missing) produces a target table that is an exact mirror of the source, applied incrementally rather than as a full overwrite. This is more efficient than overwriting when the source is large but changes are small.

# Three-way MERGE: update, insert, and delete
(target.alias('t')
    .merge(source.alias('s'), 't.meter_id = s.meter_id')
    .whenMatchedUpdate(set={
        'customer_type': col('s.customer_type'),
        'updated_at': current_timestamp(),
    })
    .whenNotMatchedInsert(values={
        'meter_id': col('s.meter_id'),
        'customer_type': col('s.customer_type'),
        'install_date': col('s.install_date'),
        'updated_at': current_timestamp(),
    })
    .whenNotMatchedBySourceDelete()  # decommissioned meters
    .execute()
)

print(f"After MERGE: {target.toDF().count():,} rows")
MERGE Performance

MERGE rewrites only the files that contain matched or affected rows. If 2,000 rows are updated across 48 files, and those rows span 6 files, MERGE rewrites only those 6 files. The other 42 files are untouched. This file-level granularity makes MERGE efficient for low-change-rate tables. For high-change-rate tables (where every file contains at least one changed row), MERGE rewrites all files and the performance is similar to a full overwrite. NordGrid's metadata MERGE touches 4–8 files per day (out of 48) and completes in under 30 seconds. The readings table's daily append writes 48 new files without touching existing ones.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use MERGE for slowly changing reference tables where a fraction of rows change per run (dimension tables, configuration tables, user profiles). Use append for fact tables where new data arrives without modifying existing rows (meter readings, events, logs). Use overwrite only when you intentionally want to replace the entire table (Gold summary tables recomputed from scratch). NordGrid's table type determines the write strategy: dimension tables use MERGE, fact tables use append, Gold aggregations use overwrite.

Every write to a Delta table creates a new version in the transaction log. Time travel lets you read any previous version of the table — by version number or by timestamp. This capability transforms incident response: instead of scrambling to find backups or re-run pipelines, you query the table as it was before the problem. NordGrid's most dramatic use of time travel occurred when a faulty Bronze-to-Silver pipeline wrote incorrect data (a misapplied type cast produced null values in the daily_kw column for all rows). The on-call engineer identified the problem at 7:30 AM, read the previous version of the Silver table to confirm it was correct, restored it in under 60 seconds, and the 8 AM dashboard SLA was met. Without time travel, the recovery would have required a full pipeline re-run — a 35-minute operation that would have breached the SLA.
Reading Previous Versions

# Read by version number
df_v2 = spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', 2).load('silver/readings_delta/')

# Read by timestamp
df_yesterday = (spark.read.format('delta')
    .option('timestampAsOf', '2026-03-21 23:59:59')
    .load('silver/readings_delta/')
)

# Compare current version with previous version
df_current = spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings_delta/')
print(f"Current version rows: {df_current.count():,}")
print(f"Previous version rows: {df_v2.count():,}")

# Find rows that changed between versions
df_diff = df_current.subtract(df_v2)
print(f"Rows added or changed: {df_diff.count():,}")
Restoring a Previous Version

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/readings_delta/')

# Check history to find the good version
dt.history(5).select('version', 'timestamp', 'operation').show(truncate=False)

# Restore to version 5 (creates a new version that is identical to version 5)
dt.restoreToVersion(5)

# Or restore to a timestamp
dt.restoreToTimestamp('2026-03-21 23:00:00')

# The restore is atomic and instant — it updates the log, not the files
# Old file versions are preserved for future time travel
Audit and Compliance Use Cases

Beyond incident response, time travel serves audit and compliance requirements. NordGrid's energy regulator requires the ability to reproduce any report as it would have appeared at the time of submission. With Delta time travel, the team can read the Gold table as of the submission timestamp and reproduce the exact numbers. This eliminates the need for separate audit snapshots — every version of every table is automatically preserved. The transaction log provides a complete audit trail: who wrote the data, when, what operation was performed, and how many rows were affected. NordGrid's compliance officer queries the history directly to verify data lineage for quarterly audits.

Time travel has a storage cost: every previous version's files are retained until vacuumed (lesson 9). For NordGrid's 3.2TB Silver table with daily overwrites, retaining 30 days of history requires approximately 30 × 3.2TB = 96TB of raw storage. In practice, the cost is much lower because Delta rewrites only changed files (via MERGE or targeted overwrite), and unchanged files are shared across versions. NordGrid's actual storage overhead for 30-day retention is approximately 15% above the current version size — the daily changes affect only a fraction of the files.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Establish a time travel retention policy before migrating to Delta. NordGrid retains 30 days of history for Silver tables (enough for incident recovery and monthly reporting), 90 days for Gold tables (enough for quarterly regulatory audits), and 7 days for Bronze tables (raw files are archived separately). The retention policy determines the VACUUM schedule, which is covered in lesson 9.

Lesson 1 categorized schema changes as additive, type-widening, or breaking. Delta Lake provides built-in support for the first two categories through schema evolution modes that integrate directly with the transaction log, and its schema enforcement on write prevents the third category from corrupting existing data. This lesson covers how to enable schema evolution on Delta writes, the difference between mergeSchema for additive changes and overwriteSchema for breaking changes, how the transaction log tracks schema history across versions, and NordGrid’s policy for handling each category of schema change in production pipelines.
mergeSchema — Additive Changes

When a source system adds a new column and your pipeline includes it in the DataFrame, Delta's default schema enforcement rejects the write because the new column is not in the table's schema. Adding the mergeSchema option tells Delta to add the new column to the table's schema automatically. Existing rows get null for the new column, and new rows include the actual values. The schema change is recorded in the transaction log as a metadata operation. NordGrid uses mergeSchema for planned additive changes: when the team knows a new column is coming (communicated by the partner or the source system team), the pipeline is updated to include the column, and mergeSchema allows the first write to extend the schema.

# Source data now includes a new column: firmware_version
df_new = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/2026-03-22/', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(df_new.columns)  # ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'firmware_version']

# Without mergeSchema: Delta rejects the write (extra column)
try:
    df_new.write.format('delta').mode('append').save('silver/readings_delta/')
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Rejected: {e}")

# With mergeSchema: Delta adds the new column to the table schema
(df_new.write
    .format('delta')
    .mode('append')
    .option('mergeSchema', 'true')  # allow additive schema changes
    .save('silver/readings_delta/')
)

# Verify: the table now has 5 columns
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings_delta/')
print(df.columns)  # ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'firmware_version']
overwriteSchema — Breaking Changes

For breaking changes — column renames, type narrowing, column removal — mergeSchema is not sufficient because the change is not additive. The overwriteSchema option replaces the table's entire schema with the new one. This is a destructive operation: existing data remains in the files, but the schema metadata changes, and any column mismatch between old files and the new schema produces null values. NordGrid uses overwriteSchema only during planned migrations (such as renaming rgn_code to region), and always with a preceding time travel backup that allows the team to roll back if the migration causes downstream failures.

# Breaking change: rename rgn_code to region in the schema
df_renamed = (spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings_delta/')
    .withColumnRenamed('rgn_code', 'region')
)

# overwriteSchema replaces the table's schema entirely
(df_renamed.write
    .format('delta')
    .mode('overwrite')
    .option('overwriteSchema', 'true')  # replace schema, not merge
    .save('silver/readings_delta/')
)
Reviewing Schema History

Delta’s transaction log records every schema change as a metadata operation alongside the data changes in the same version entry. You can review the schema history by querying the table’s history and comparing the schemas of different versions using time travel reads. NordGrid’s schema governance process requires that every schema change — whether additive or breaking — is documented in the transaction log with a descriptive commit message, linked to a change management ticket, and reviewed by the data platform team before the pipeline that introduces the change is deployed to production.

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/readings_delta/')

# Show history focusing on schema changes
history = dt.history()
history.filter("operation = 'SET TBLPROPERTIES' OR operation = 'WRITE'") \
    .select('version', 'timestamp', 'operation', 'operationParameters') \
    .show(truncate=False)

# Compare schemas between two versions
df_v0 = spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', 0).load('silver/readings_delta/')
df_current = spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings_delta/')

v0_cols = set(df_v0.columns)
current_cols = set(df_current.columns)
print(f"Columns added: {current_cols - v0_cols}")
print(f"Columns removed: {v0_cols - current_cols}")

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's schema change policy: additive changes use mergeSchema and are deployed without downtime. Type-widening changes are tested in staging, then deployed with mergeSchema and a post-write validation step. Breaking changes use overwriteSchema, require a time travel backup, a migration script, downstream consumer notification, and a coordinated deployment window. Every schema change is logged in the team's change management system with the classification (additive/widening/breaking) and the Delta version number.

Delta Lake retains all previous file versions until you explicitly remove them. Every MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE, and overwrite creates new files and marks old files as removed in the transaction log — but the old files remain on disk. Over time, this accumulates storage: NordGrid's Silver table, with daily overwrites for 30 days, had 40 versions of the data files consuming 4.8TB instead of the current version's 3.2TB. VACUUM removes files that are no longer referenced by any version within the retention period. OPTIMIZE compacts small files into larger ones. Z-ORDER co-locates related data for faster filtering. Together, these three maintenance operations keep Delta tables performant and storage costs under control.
VACUUM — Cleaning Up Old Files

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/readings_delta/')

# Dry run: show which files would be deleted (does not delete anything)
dt.vacuum(168)  # 168 hours = 7 days retention

# Actually delete files older than the retention period
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.vacuum.parallelDelete.enabled', 'true')
dt.vacuum(168)  # deletes files not referenced by any version in the last 7 days

# WARNING: after VACUUM, time travel to versions older than the retention period
# will fail because the files have been deleted

# NordGrid retention periods:
# Silver: VACUUM(720)  = 30 days
# Gold:   VACUUM(2160) = 90 days
# Bronze: VACUUM(168)  = 7 days

VACUUM has a critical interaction with time travel: after vacuuming with a 7-day retention, any time travel query to a version older than 7 days fails because the underlying files have been deleted. The retention period must be at least as long as your oldest time travel need. NordGrid's Silver retention is 30 days because the monthly billing reconciliation queries data from the beginning of the month. The Gold retention is 90 days because the quarterly regulatory audit requires access to any day within the quarter. Setting the retention too short saves storage but loses the ability to recover from incidents discovered after the retention window closes.
OPTIMIZE — File Compaction

OPTIMIZE rewrites small files into fewer, larger files. This is Delta's equivalent of the manual compaction job from Module 2, lesson 7, but with two advantages: OPTIMIZE is atomic (it uses the transaction log, so concurrent readers are never affected), and it can be combined with Z-ORDER for data clustering. NordGrid runs OPTIMIZE weekly on the Silver table and daily on Gold tables that receive frequent MERGE operations. The OPTIMIZE command targets files smaller than a configurable threshold (default 1GB) and merges them, producing files in the 256MB–1GB range.

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/readings_delta/')

# Basic OPTIMIZE: compact small files
dt.optimize().executeCompaction()

# OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER: compact AND co-locate data by the specified columns
# Z-ORDER places rows with similar values of the specified columns in the same files,
# enabling Parquet's min/max statistics to skip more row groups during filtered reads.
dt.optimize().executeZOrderBy('region', 'date')

# Verify: check the number of files before and after
from pyspark.sql.functions import input_file_name, countDistinct
file_count = (spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings_delta/')
    .select(input_file_name().alias('file'))
    .agg(countDistinct('file').alias('files'))
    .collect()[0]['files']
)
print(f"Files after OPTIMIZE: {file_count}")
NordGrid’s Maintenance Schedule

blocks.title: VACUUM is irreversible
blocks.content: Once VACUUM deletes a file, it cannot be recovered. Time travel to versions that referenced the deleted file will fail with a FileNotFoundException. Always verify your retention period before running VACUUM in production. NordGrid's safety practice: the first VACUUM on any table is a dry run (log which files would be deleted without actually deleting them). The engineer reviews the file list, confirms the retention period is correct, and then runs the actual VACUUM. The Airflow DAG for VACUUM includes a Slack notification with the file count and size before executing the delete.

This lesson synthesizes the module into NordGrid's Delta Lake migration plan and operational playbook. The migration is phased: Silver tables first (the highest-value targets for ACID transactions and time travel), then Gold tables (for MERGE support on dimension-like aggregations), with Bronze remaining as plain Parquet (the raw archive format where Delta's features add cost without corresponding benefit). The plan covers the technical migration steps, the transition period where both Parquet and Delta coexist, the schema evolution policy that governs how changes propagate across the platform, and the monitoring dashboard that tracks Delta table health.
Migration Plan

rows: Convert in place with DeltaTable.convertToDelta(). Update pipeline write format from parquet to delta. Deploy schema enforcement.
Gold monthly summary, Gold billing detail
Convert in place. Replace overwrite-based Gold writes with MERGE where applicable. Add Z-ORDER on query columns.
Deploy OPTIMIZE + VACUUM Airflow DAGs. Set up Delta table health dashboard. Train the analytics team on time travel queries.
Bronze is append-only raw data. Delta's transactional features add overhead without benefit. Plain Parquet is retained.

blocks.content: Schema Evolution Policy

NordGrid's schema evolution policy defines the process for each change category in the Delta Lake era. Additive changes (new columns): the pipeline is updated to include the new column, the write uses option('mergeSchema', 'true'), the change is documented in the change log, and downstream consumers are notified. The pipeline's schema enforcement is updated to include the new column in the expected schema. Type-widening changes: tested in staging with the new type, deployed with mergeSchema, and a post-write validation asserts that no existing values were corrupted by the widening. Breaking changes: require a scheduled migration window, use overwriteSchema, include a time travel checkpoint, downstream consumer notification, and a rollback plan. Every schema change is tied to a JIRA ticket and reviewed by the data platform team before deployment.
Monitoring Dashboard

NordGrid's Delta table health dashboard tracks five metrics per table: current version number (detects stale tables where the pipeline stopped writing), file count (detects small file accumulation between OPTIMIZE runs), total size in bytes (detects unexpected growth from failed VACUUM or orphaned files), time since last OPTIMIZE (triggers an alert if it exceeds the schedule), and time since last VACUUM (triggers an alert if retention period is at risk). The dashboard reads from the Delta transaction log and the filesystem, requiring no additional instrumentation in the pipeline code. It was the first monitoring component deployed during Phase 3 because it catches the maintenance problems that are easy to overlook and expensive to fix retroactively.
What Comes Next

Modules 1–4 have given you the performance and architectural foundations of mid-level PySpark: execution plan analysis, partitioning strategy, join optimization, and Delta Lake. Module 5 shifts to pipeline design patterns — the structural decisions that determine whether a pipeline is resilient to failures, idempotent across retries, and capable of handling the operational realities of late-arriving data, backfills, and slowly changing dimensions. Where Module 4 addressed what happens when the data format changes, Module 5 addresses what happens when the pipeline itself needs to handle the messy reality of production data flows.

blocks.title: Module 4 complete
blocks.content: You now understand why schemas change and how to classify changes by impact. You can use schema merge for historical queries, enforce schemas defensively at both read and write time, and test schema contracts in CI. You can read and write Delta tables, perform MERGE upserts, use time travel for incident recovery and auditing, evolve schemas with mergeSchema and overwriteSchema, and maintain tables with OPTIMIZE, Z-ORDER, and VACUUM. NordGrid's Delta Lake migration plan, schema evolution policy, and maintenance schedule provide the operational framework for applying these capabilities in production.

chapters.title: Pipeline Design Patterns
chapters.description: Design idempotent, incremental, and fault-tolerant pipelines that handle real-world production concerns: late-arriving data, backfills, slowly changing dimensions, and multi-stage error recovery.

blocks.content: A pipeline is idempotent when running it twice with the same input produces the same result as running it once. This sounds obvious until you consider what happens in production. NordGrid's daily Bronze ingestion pipeline used append mode. The pipeline ran at 6 AM, appended 1.2 million readings to the Bronze table, and succeeded. At 6:45 AM, the Airflow scheduler retried the pipeline after a transient cluster error — the error had already resolved, but the retry was automatic. The append ran again, adding another 1.2 million identical readings. The Bronze table now had 2.4 million rows for one day instead of 1.2 million. Every downstream aggregate — daily consumption, monthly billing, regional summaries — was doubled. The pipeline was correct. It was not idempotent. And the duplication was not discovered until the billing team noticed charges that were exactly twice the expected amount.
Why Retries Happen

In production, pipelines retry for many reasons: transient cluster errors (a node restarted), network timeouts (the write to cloud storage timed out but actually succeeded), orchestrator retries (Airflow's default retry policy re-runs failed tasks), manual re-runs (an engineer re-triggers a pipeline to fix a data issue without realizing it already ran), and catch-up runs (after an outage, the scheduler runs multiple days' pipelines back-to-back). Every one of these scenarios runs your pipeline more than once for the same input period. If your pipeline is not idempotent, every retry risks data duplication, data loss, or data corruption. Idempotency is not an optimization — it is a reliability requirement.
The Three Idempotency Patterns

rows: write.mode('overwrite') replaces the entire output on every run
Simplest. No duplicates possible. Always produces correct output.
Rewrites all data even if nothing changed. Dangerous if pointed at wrong path.
Gold summary tables (small, fully recomputed each run)
Delete the target partition, then write new data. Atomic in Delta.
Only rewrites the affected partition. Safe for partitioned tables.
Not atomic in plain Parquet (delete + write = two operations). Requires partition alignment.
Silver readings partitioned by date (overwrite one day's partition)
Delta Lake MERGE: update matched rows, insert new rows
Only touches changed rows. Most efficient for low-change-rate tables.
Requires Delta Lake. MERGE logic can be complex for multi-column keys.

blocks.content: Implementing Delete-Then-Insert

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit
from datetime import date

def write_idempotent_partition(df, target_path, partition_col, partition_value, format='delta'):
    """Idempotent write: replace exactly one partition's data.
    
    If the pipeline runs twice for the same partition_value,
    the second run overwrites the first — no duplicates.
    """
    (df
        .filter(col(partition_col) == lit(partition_value))
        .write
        .format(format)
        .mode('overwrite')
        .option('replaceWhere', f"{partition_col} = '{partition_value}'")
        .save(target_path)
    )

# Usage: write today's readings idempotently
today = date.today()
df_today = transform_bronze_to_silver(spark, today)
write_idempotent_partition(
    df_today, 'silver/readings_delta/', 'date', str(today)
)
# Running this twice for the same date produces the same result — idempotent

The replaceWhere option is the key: it tells Delta to delete all rows matching the condition and then insert the new rows, atomically within a single transaction. If the pipeline fails mid-write, the transaction is rolled back and the previous version is preserved. If the pipeline runs twice, the second run replaces the first run's data with identical data — the result is the same as running once. This is the idempotency guarantee that append mode cannot provide. NordGrid uses replaceWhere for every Silver table write where the data is partitioned by a date or batch identifier.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Choose the idempotency pattern based on the table's characteristics. Overwrite for small tables that are fully recomputed (Gold summaries). Delete-then-insert with replaceWhere for partitioned fact tables (Silver readings by date). MERGE for dimension tables with low change rates (meter metadata). NordGrid's pipeline template requires every pipeline to declare its idempotency strategy in the YAML configuration: overwrite, replace_where, or merge. If the strategy is not declared, the CI pipeline rejects the PR.

The simplest pipeline design processes all data from scratch on every run: read the entire Bronze archive, transform everything, write the full Silver table. This is correct but inefficient. NordGrid's Bronze archive holds 3.2TB of three-year historical data. Processing all of it every day takes 35 minutes and shuffles terabytes across the network. But only 3GB of new data arrives daily — 0.1% of the archive. Incremental processing reads and transforms only the new data, reducing the daily pipeline from 35 minutes to 90 seconds. The challenge is knowing which data is new. This lesson covers the high-water-mark pattern that NordGrid uses to track processing progress and filter to only unprocessed data.
The High-Water-Mark Pattern

The high-water-mark (HWM) pattern tracks the maximum value of an ordering column (typically a date or a monotonically increasing ID) that the pipeline has already processed. On each run, the pipeline reads only rows where the ordering column exceeds the high-water mark. After successful processing, it updates the high-water mark to the new maximum. NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses the date column as the watermark: if the current HWM is 2026-03-21, the pipeline reads only Bronze data where date > '2026-03-21', processes it, writes the Silver output, and updates the HWM to 2026-03-22. The next run starts from 2026-03-22.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, max as spark_max, lit
from datetime import date
import json

HWM_FILE = 'state/silver_readings_hwm.json'

def read_hwm():
    """Read the current high-water mark from the state file."""
    try:
        with open(HWM_FILE) as f:
            return json.load(f)['hwm_date']
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return '1970-01-01'  # no state = process everything

def write_hwm(new_hwm):
    """Update the high-water mark after successful processing."""
    with open(HWM_FILE, 'w') as f:
        json.dump({'hwm_date': new_hwm, 'updated_at': str(date.today())}, f)

# Incremental pipeline
hwm = read_hwm()
print(f"High-water mark: {hwm}")

# Read only new data
df_new = (spark.read.format('delta').load('bronze/readings/')
    .filter(col('date') > lit(hwm))
)

row_count = df_new.count()
if row_count == 0:
    print("No new data. Exiting.")
else:
    # Transform and write
    df_silver = transform_bronze_to_silver(df_new)
    write_idempotent_partition(df_silver, 'silver/readings_delta/', 'date', str(date.today()))
    
    # Update HWM only after successful write
    new_hwm = df_new.agg(spark_max('date')).collect()[0][0]
    write_hwm(str(new_hwm))
    print(f"Processed {row_count:,} rows. New HWM: {new_hwm}")
HWM Storage Options

The high-water mark must be stored durably outside the pipeline's ephemeral execution environment. NordGrid evaluated three options: a JSON file on shared storage (simple but no concurrency control), a database row in PostgreSQL (durable and supports concurrent access), and a Delta table with a single row (uses the same infrastructure as the data pipeline). The team chose the Delta table approach because it provides ACID guarantees, is readable from both PySpark and SQL, and integrates with the existing monitoring dashboard. The HWM table has three columns: pipeline_name, hwm_value, and updated_at. Each pipeline updates its own row after a successful run.

A critical design decision: the HWM must be updated only after the downstream write succeeds. If the pipeline reads new data, updates the HWM, and then fails during the write, the HWM points past the unprocessed data — and the next run skips it. This is the 'at-least-once' guarantee: always update the HWM after the write, never before. If the pipeline writes successfully but crashes before updating the HWM, the next run reprocesses the same data — which is safe because the write is idempotent (lesson 1). The combination of idempotent writes and post-write HWM updates produces exactly-once semantics in practice.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Combine idempotent writes (lesson 1) with incremental processing (this lesson) for the standard production pipeline pattern: read new data since the last HWM, transform it, write idempotently to the target partition, update the HWM after the write succeeds. This combination handles retries (idempotency prevents duplicates), failures (post-write HWM prevents data loss), and normal operation (incremental processing limits the data volume to the daily delta). NordGrid's pipeline template implements this pattern as a base class that all pipelines inherit.

Incremental processing assumes that data arrives in order: today's pipeline processes today's data. But production data does not always arrive on time. NordGrid's meters communicate via cellular networks, and communication failures cause readings to arrive 1–3 days late. The March 20th pipeline processes all readings with date = '2026-03-20'. On March 22nd, 3,000 readings from March 20th arrive — their meters had intermittent connectivity. The March 20th partition has already been written and the HWM has moved past it. The standard incremental pipeline skips these readings because their date is below the HWM. The readings are lost unless the pipeline has a late-data handling strategy.
The Late Data Detection Pattern

The first step is detecting that late data exists. NordGrid's Bronze ingestion pipeline tags every batch with an ingestion_timestamp — the wall-clock time when the data arrived. Late data has a date column (the business date of the reading) that is earlier than the ingestion_timestamp minus one day. A daily scan of the Bronze table identifies late records: rows where date < ingestion_timestamp - interval 1 day. These rows are flagged as late and routed to a separate reprocessing path.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_date, datediff, lit

# Identify late-arriving data in Bronze
df_bronze = spark.read.format('delta').load('bronze/readings/')

# Late data: business date is more than 1 day before ingestion
df_late = (df_bronze
    .filter(datediff(col('ingestion_timestamp').cast('date'), col('date')) > 1)
    .filter(col('ingestion_timestamp').cast('date') == current_date())  # arrived today
)

late_count = df_late.count()
print(f"Late-arriving records found today: {late_count:,}")

if late_count > 0:
    # Group by business date to see which partitions need reprocessing
    df_late.groupBy('date').count().orderBy('date').show()
    # Reprocess affected partitions
    for row in df_late.select('date').distinct().collect():
        reprocess_partition(spark, str(row['date']))
The Reprocessing Window

NordGrid's late data policy defines a reprocessing window: any data that arrives within 7 days of its business date is automatically reprocessed by updating the affected Silver partition using the replaceWhere pattern from lesson 1. Data that arrives more than 7 days late is logged and flagged for manual review because it may indicate a systemic data quality issue rather than a simple communication delay. The reprocessing window is a business decision, not a technical one: 7 days was chosen because 99.8% of NordGrid's late data arrives within 3 days, and the remaining 0.2% that arrives between 3 and 7 days is small enough to reprocess cheaply. Beyond 7 days, the cost of automatic reprocessing (re-aggregating all downstream Gold tables) exceeds the value of the late data.

from datetime import date, timedelta

def reprocess_partition(spark, business_date_str):
    """Reprocess a Silver partition to incorporate late-arriving data.
    
    Reads ALL Bronze data for the given date (including late arrivals),
    transforms it, and overwrites the Silver partition idempotently.
    """
    # Read all Bronze data for this date, including late arrivals
    df_all = (spark.read.format('delta').load('bronze/readings/')
        .filter(col('date') == lit(business_date_str))
    )
    
    # Standard Bronze-to-Silver transformation
    df_silver = transform_bronze_to_silver(df_all)
    
    # Idempotent partition overwrite
    (df_silver.write
        .format('delta')
        .mode('overwrite')
        .option('replaceWhere', f"date = '{business_date_str}'")
        .save('silver/readings_delta/')
    )
    
    print(f"Reprocessed {business_date_str}: {df_all.count():,} total rows (including late)")

# Daily late-data handler: reprocess any dates with new late arrivals
for day_offset in range(1, 8):  # check the last 7 days
    check_date = date.today() - timedelta(days=day_offset)
    if has_new_late_data(spark, str(check_date)):  # custom function
        reprocess_partition(spark, str(check_date))

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's daily pipeline has two stages: the forward-processing stage (new data via HWM) and the late-data stage (reprocessing affected partitions from the last 7 days). Both stages are idempotent. The forward stage runs first, then the late-data handler checks each of the last 7 days for new arrivals and reprocesses as needed. On a typical day, 0–2 partitions need reprocessing, adding 30–60 seconds to the pipeline. The Airflow DAG logs which partitions were reprocessed and how many late records were incorporated.

Late data arrives because the source was delayed. Backfills happen because you need to reprocess historical data with new logic. A pipeline bug is fixed and the last 30 days of Silver data need to be regenerated. A new column is added to the Silver schema and all historical data must be transformed to include it. A business rule changes retroactively — NordGrid's tiered pricing formula was updated, and all billing aggregates for the current quarter need recalculation. Each scenario requires reprocessing a range of historical dates through the pipeline, and doing this efficiently, safely, and without disrupting the daily batch is the challenge this lesson addresses.
Date-Range Parameterized Pipelines

The foundation of backfill capability is a pipeline that accepts the processing date as a parameter rather than hardcoding today's date. NordGrid's pipeline template accepts a --date argument (or a date range --start-date and --end-date) that determines which data to process. The daily batch passes today's date. A backfill passes a historical date range. The transformation logic is identical in both cases — only the input date differs. This parameterization is a design-time decision: if the pipeline is written with date.today() hardcoded, backfilling requires modifying code. If the pipeline accepts a date parameter, backfilling requires only a different argument.

import argparse
from datetime import date, timedelta

def parse_args():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='NordGrid Silver pipeline')
    parser.add_argument('--start-date', type=str, default=str(date.today()),
                        help='Start date (inclusive) in YYYY-MM-DD format')
    parser.add_argument('--end-date', type=str, default=str(date.today()),
                        help='End date (inclusive) in YYYY-MM-DD format')
    return parser.parse_args()

def main():
    args = parse_args()
    spark = create_spark_session('nordgrid-silver-pipeline')
    
    try:
        # Process each date in the range
        current = date.fromisoformat(args.start_date)
        end = date.fromisoformat(args.end_date)
        
        while current <= end:
            process_date(spark, current)  # idempotent per-date processing
            current += timedelta(days=1)
    finally:
        spark.stop()

# Daily batch:    python pipeline.py --start-date 2026-03-22
# 30-day backfill: python pipeline.py --start-date 2026-02-20 --end-date 2026-03-22
Backfill Orchestration

Processing a 90-day backfill as a single spark-submit with a date loop is simple but has two problems: if the job fails on day 45, you must restart from day 1 (or manually determine which days completed), and the job monopolizes cluster resources for the entire duration. Orchestration tools like Airflow solve both problems: each date becomes a separate task in a DAG, and Airflow tracks which tasks completed. If the backfill fails on day 45, Airflow resumes from day 45. The tasks can run with limited parallelism (4 dates at a time instead of 90), leaving cluster capacity for other jobs. NordGrid's backfill DAG accepts a date range and generates one task per date, with a concurrency limit of 4.
Downstream Propagation

Reprocessing Silver does not automatically update Gold. If you backfill 30 days of Silver readings, the Gold monthly summary still reflects the old Silver data until you re-run the Gold pipeline for the affected periods. NordGrid's backfill runbook requires three steps: (1) backfill Silver for the affected date range, (2) identify which Gold tables read from the affected Silver partitions, (3) re-run those Gold pipelines for the affected periods. The runbook lists every Silver-to-Gold dependency so the engineer does not forget a downstream table. For the tiered pricing retroactive change, the backfill touched Silver readings for 90 days, Gold monthly billing for 3 months, and Gold quarterly regulatory for 1 quarter — four pipeline runs in sequence.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Every pipeline should accept date parameters from day one, even if you do not plan to backfill. The cost is one argparse block. The benefit is the ability to reprocess any historical period without modifying code. NordGrid's pipeline template includes date parameterization as a built-in feature: every pipeline inherits --start-date and --end-date arguments. When a backfill is needed, the engineer runs the same pipeline with different dates. No code changes. No emergency PRs. No hotfixes.

NordGrid's meter metadata table holds 1.2 million rows with columns like customer_type, substation_id, and install_date. These attributes change slowly: a meter's customer_type changes when the property changes ownership (perhaps 5,000 times per year across the fleet), and substation assignments change during grid reconfigurations (perhaps 200 times per year). The billing team needs to know what each meter's customer_type was at the time of each reading, not what it is today. If meter M-001 was 'residential' in January and changed to 'commercial' in February, January's billing rate should be residential and February's should be commercial. This is the slowly changing dimension (SCD) problem, and the solution determines whether historical reports produce accurate or retroactively incorrect results.
SCD Type 1: Overwrite

SCD Type 1 is the simplest approach: when an attribute changes, overwrite the old value with the new one. The dimension table always reflects the current state. This is what a standard MERGE with whenMatchedUpdate does: meter M-001's customer_type is updated from 'residential' to 'commercial', and the old value is gone. SCD Type 1 is appropriate when historical accuracy is not required — for example, updating a meter's firmware_version, where only the current version matters. NordGrid uses SCD Type 1 for operational attributes (firmware, signal strength, last_communication_date) that are queried for their current value, not their historical value.
SCD Type 2: History Tracking

SCD Type 2 preserves the full history of every change. Instead of overwriting the old value, the pipeline marks the old row as inactive and inserts a new row with the updated value. Each row has effective_from and effective_to date columns that define when that version was valid. Meter M-001 has two rows: one with customer_type = 'residential', effective_from = '2020-06-15', effective_to = '2026-02-01', and another with customer_type = 'commercial', effective_from = '2026-02-01', effective_to = '9999-12-31' (a sentinel indicating the current version). To find the customer_type at any historical point, you query where the date falls between effective_from and effective_to.

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_date, lit, when

target = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'silver/meters_scd2/')
source = spark.read.csv('bronze/crm_export/2026-03-22/', header=True, schema=crm_schema)

# Step 1: Identify changed rows (current version in target does not match source)
current_target = target.toDF().filter(col('effective_to') == '9999-12-31')
changed = (current_target.alias('t')
    .join(source.alias('s'), 't.meter_id = s.meter_id')
    .filter(col('t.customer_type') != col('s.customer_type'))
    .select('t.meter_id')
)

# Step 2: Close the old version (set effective_to = today)
(target.alias('t')
    .merge(changed.alias('c'), 't.meter_id = c.meter_id AND t.effective_to = "9999-12-31"')
    .whenMatchedUpdate(set={
        'effective_to': current_date()
    })
    .execute()
)

# Step 3: Insert new versions for changed meters
new_versions = (source.alias('s')
    .join(changed.alias('c'), 's.meter_id = c.meter_id')
    .select(
        col('s.meter_id'),
        col('s.customer_type'),
        col('s.substation_id'),
        current_date().alias('effective_from'),
        lit('9999-12-31').cast('date').alias('effective_to'),
    )
)

new_versions.write.format('delta').mode('append').save('silver/meters_scd2/')

# Step 4: Insert genuinely new meters (not in target at all)
(target.alias('t')
    .merge(source.alias('s'), 't.meter_id = s.meter_id')
    .whenNotMatchedInsert(values={
        'meter_id': col('s.meter_id'),
        'customer_type': col('s.customer_type'),
        'substation_id': col('s.substation_id'),
        'effective_from': current_date(),
        'effective_to': lit('9999-12-31').cast('date'),
    })
    .execute()
)
Choosing Between SCD Types

left.label: SCD Type 1 (Overwrite)
points: Use for: firmware, signal, operational attributes

right.label: SCD Type 2 (History)
points: Full history preserved with date ranges
Multiple rows per entity (one per version)
Complex: close old row, insert new row, handle new entities
Use for: customer_type, pricing tier, anything billed or audited

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's meter metadata uses both SCD types in the same table pipeline. Operational attributes (firmware_version, signal_strength) use Type 1 — overwritten in place via MERGE. Billing-relevant attributes (customer_type, substation_id) use Type 2 — history-tracked with effective dates. The pipeline runs as two MERGE operations in sequence: Type 1 for operational columns, then Type 2 for billing columns. This hybrid approach keeps the implementation simple for attributes that do not need history while providing full audit trails for attributes that do.

NordGrid's daily pipeline has three stages: Bronze-to-Silver, Silver-to-Gold-summary, and Silver-to-Gold-billing. Each stage reads from the previous stage's output and writes to its own target. When all three stages succeed, the pipeline is complete. The failure scenario is more complex: what happens when stage 1 (Silver) succeeds, stage 2 (Gold summary) succeeds, but stage 3 (Gold billing) fails? The Silver and Gold summary tables have new data, but the Gold billing table does not. The system is in a partially updated state. If the pipeline re-runs from the beginning, it reprocesses Silver and Gold summary unnecessarily. If it skips to stage 3, it must verify that stages 1 and 2 actually succeeded. This lesson covers the checkpoint pattern that enables safe, efficient recovery from mid-pipeline failures.
The Completion Marker Pattern

Each stage writes a completion marker after its write succeeds: a small file or database record that indicates the stage completed for a specific date. Before executing, each stage checks whether its completion marker already exists. If it does, the stage is skipped. If it does not, the stage runs. This allows the pipeline to resume from the failed stage without re-executing completed stages. The markers are keyed by pipeline name, stage name, and processing date, so they work correctly for backfills (different dates can be in different completion states) and for retries (the same date can be retried without reprocessing completed stages).

from datetime import date

class StageCheckpoint:
    """Track stage completion using a Delta state table."""
    
    def __init__(self, spark, state_table='state.pipeline_checkpoints'):
        self.spark = spark
        self.state_table = state_table
    
    def is_complete(self, pipeline, stage, process_date):
        """Check if a stage has already completed for this date."""
        result = self.spark.sql(f"""
            SELECT 1 FROM {self.state_table}
            WHERE pipeline = '{pipeline}'
              AND stage = '{stage}'
              AND process_date = '{process_date}'
              AND status = 'SUCCESS'
        """)
        return result.count() > 0
    
    def mark_complete(self, pipeline, stage, process_date, row_count=0):
        """Record successful completion of a stage."""
        self.spark.sql(f"""
            INSERT INTO {self.state_table}
            VALUES ('{pipeline}', '{stage}', '{process_date}', 'SUCCESS',
                    {row_count}, current_timestamp())
        """)

# Usage in the pipeline
checkpoint = StageCheckpoint(spark)
process_date = '2026-03-22'

if not checkpoint.is_complete('daily_meters', 'bronze_to_silver', process_date):
    run_bronze_to_silver(spark, process_date)
    checkpoint.mark_complete('daily_meters', 'bronze_to_silver', process_date)
else:
    print(f"Skipping bronze_to_silver: already complete for {process_date}")

if not checkpoint.is_complete('daily_meters', 'silver_to_gold_summary', process_date):
    run_silver_to_gold_summary(spark, process_date)
    checkpoint.mark_complete('daily_meters', 'silver_to_gold_summary', process_date)

if not checkpoint.is_complete('daily_meters', 'silver_to_gold_billing', process_date):
    run_silver_to_gold_billing(spark, process_date)
    checkpoint.mark_complete('daily_meters', 'silver_to_gold_billing', process_date)

If the pipeline fails on the Gold billing stage and is re-run, the checkpoint for Silver and Gold summary already shows SUCCESS. The pipeline skips straight to Gold billing, saving the 90 seconds of Silver processing and the 45 seconds of Gold summary processing. For a backfill of 90 dates, this checkpoint pattern prevents re-executing hundreds of already-completed stage runs. The completion marker table also serves as an operational dashboard: the team can query it to see which dates have completed all three stages and which are stuck on a specific stage.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's checkpoint table records pipeline name, stage name, date, status (SUCCESS/FAILED), row count, and timestamp. The Airflow DAG queries this table before each task to decide whether to skip. A separate monitoring query checks for dates where some stages are SUCCESS but the final stage is not — these are stuck pipelines that need investigation. The checkpoint table is the single source of truth for pipeline progress, replacing the ad-hoc log-file-checking that the team used before.

NordGrid's early pipelines hardcoded everything: table paths, schema definitions, quality thresholds, spark-submit configuration, and even the list of regions to process. Every environment change required a code change. Moving from development to staging meant editing 12 path strings in the pipeline script. Adding a new region meant modifying a hardcoded list and deploying a new version. These code changes introduced bugs (a typo in a staging path pointed to production data) and required the full CI/CD cycle for what should have been a configuration change. Configuration-driven pipelines extract all environment-specific and business-rule-specific values into external configuration files, leaving the pipeline code generic and reusable across environments.
The YAML Configuration Pattern

import yaml
import os

def load_config():
    """Load pipeline configuration from YAML.
    
    The PIPELINE_ENV environment variable selects the config file:
    dev.yaml, staging.yaml, or production.yaml.
    """
    env = os.environ.get('PIPELINE_ENV', 'dev')
    config_path = f'config/{env}.yaml'
    
    with open(config_path) as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    
    print(f"Loaded config for environment: {env}")
    return config

# Usage
config = load_config()

# All paths, thresholds, and settings come from config
bronze_path = config['tables']['bronze_readings']['path']
silver_path = config['tables']['silver_readings']['path']
shuffle_partitions = config['spark']['shuffle_partitions']
quality_null_threshold = config['quality']['max_null_rate']
regions = config['business']['regions']

# config/production.yaml
tables:
  bronze_readings:
    path: s3://nordgrid-prod/bronze/readings/
    format: delta
  silver_readings:
    path: s3://nordgrid-prod/silver/readings/
    format: delta
    partition_by: [region, date]

spark:
  app_name: nordgrid-daily-meters-silver
  shuffle_partitions: 48
  broadcast_threshold: 52428800  # 50MB

quality:
  max_null_rate: 0.005        # 0.5% max null values in daily_kw
  min_row_count: 1000000      # at least 1M rows expected daily
  max_row_count: 1500000      # at most 1.5M rows expected daily

business:
  regions: [NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, NE, NW, SE, SW, CENTRAL, COASTAL, MOUNTAIN, VALLEY]
  reprocessing_window_days: 7
  pricing_tiers:
    low: {max_kw: 500, rate: 0.08}
    medium: {max_kw: 2000, rate: 0.12}
    high: {max_kw: 4000, rate: 0.18}
    critical: {rate: 0.25}
What Belongs in Configuration

rows: Different per environment (small cluster in dev, large in prod)
Max null rate, expected row count range, value bounds
Change without code release when data volume grows
Pricing tiers, region list, reprocessing window
Business changes should not require pipeline code changes

blocks.content: NordGrid maintains three config files: dev.yaml (local paths, 16 shuffle partitions, relaxed quality thresholds), staging.yaml (staging cluster paths, 48 shuffle partitions, production quality thresholds), and production.yaml (production paths, 48 shuffle partitions, strict quality thresholds). The pipeline code never references a specific environment — it reads everything from config. Deploying to a new environment is adding a new YAML file, not modifying code. When the business team adds a 13th region, the change is a single line in production.yaml, reviewed and merged without touching any pipeline logic.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's configuration rule: if a value might differ between environments or might change without a code change, it belongs in YAML configuration, not in the pipeline script. The only things that belong in code are transformation logic (the sequence of filter, join, aggregate operations) and structural patterns (the checkpoint logic, the idempotency strategy). Everything else — paths, thresholds, settings, business parameters — is configuration. The CI pipeline validates that every YAML file parses correctly and contains all required keys.

An idempotent, incremental, configuration-driven pipeline is mechanically robust. But mechanical robustness does not prevent data quality problems: a source system that sends all null values, a filter that accidentally excludes 90% of rows, or a join that inflates row counts through duplicate keys. These problems produce output that passes schema validation but contains wrong data. Data quality gates validate the content of the output — not just its structure — between pipeline stages. If a gate fails, the pipeline halts before bad data propagates downstream. NordGrid places quality gates between every major stage boundary: after Silver writes and before Gold writes.
The Quality Gate Pattern

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class QualityCheckResult:
    check_name: str
    passed: bool
    actual_value: float
    threshold: float
    message: str

def run_quality_gates(spark, table_path, config) -> List[QualityCheckResult]:
    """Run all quality checks on a table and return results."""
    df = spark.read.format('delta').load(table_path)
    results = []
    total_rows = df.count()
    
    # Check 1: Row count within expected range
    min_rows = config['quality']['min_row_count']
    max_rows = config['quality']['max_row_count']
    results.append(QualityCheckResult(
        'row_count_range', min_rows <= total_rows <= max_rows, total_rows, min_rows,
        f"Row count {total_rows:,} (expected {min_rows:,}-{max_rows:,})"
    ))
    
    # Check 2: Null rate on critical columns
    null_count = df.filter(df['daily_kw'].isNull()).count()
    null_rate = null_count / total_rows if total_rows > 0 else 0
    max_null = config['quality']['max_null_rate']
    results.append(QualityCheckResult(
        'daily_kw_null_rate', null_rate <= max_null, null_rate, max_null,
        f"Null rate {null_rate:.4f} (max {max_null})"
    ))
    
    # Check 3: Value range
    out_of_range = df.filter((df['daily_kw'] < 0) | (df['daily_kw'] > 50000)).count()
    oor_rate = out_of_range / total_rows if total_rows > 0 else 0
    results.append(QualityCheckResult(
        'daily_kw_range', oor_rate < 0.001, oor_rate, 0.001,
        f"Out-of-range rate {oor_rate:.6f} (max 0.001)"
    ))
    
    return results

# Usage: halt pipeline if any gate fails
results = run_quality_gates(spark, 'silver/readings_delta/', config)
failed = [r for r in results if not r.passed]

if failed:
    for r in failed:
        print(f"FAILED: {r.check_name} — {r.message}")
    raise RuntimeError(f"{len(failed)} quality gate(s) failed. Gold write blocked.")
else:
    print("All quality gates passed. Proceeding to Gold write.")
    run_silver_to_gold(spark, config)
What Quality Gates Check

rows: Fraction of null values in critical columns
No duplicate rows on the dedup key
All foreign keys exist in reference tables
100% of meter_ids in Silver exist in meters metadata
All 12 regions have at least 50K rows

blocks.content: NordGrid's quality gates caught three production issues in the first month: a source system that sent all-null daily_kw values for one region (caught by the null rate check), a join that produced 2.4 million rows instead of 1.2 million due to a duplicate key in the meters table (caught by the row count check), and a filter bug that excluded readings below 10 kW instead of below 0 kW (caught by the row count check — the count dropped to 800K). In each case, the gate blocked the Gold write and alerted the team, preventing bad data from reaching dashboards and billing systems.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Quality gates are not optional — they are the reason NordGrid's stakeholders trust the data. The quality thresholds are defined in the YAML configuration (lesson 7), and the gate results are written to the checkpoint table (lesson 6) so they are queryable. A Slack alert fires on any gate failure, and the on-call engineer investigates before manually approving the Gold write. The gates run in under 30 seconds (counting and filtering on a 1.2M-row table is fast) and save hours of downstream data correction when they catch a problem.

When a stakeholder questions a number in a Gold table — 'Why does the NORTH region show 12% higher consumption this month?' — the debugging process traces backward: which Silver partitions contributed to this Gold row, which Bronze files fed those Silver partitions, when were they processed, and what version of the pipeline logic was running? Without metadata, this investigation requires manual log searching, timestamp comparison, and guesswork. With pipeline metadata embedded in the output tables, the investigation is a single SQL query. This lesson covers the practice of tagging every output row with processing metadata that enables end-to-end traceability from Gold tables back to Bronze source files.
Metadata Columns

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit, current_timestamp, input_file_name
import importlib.metadata

def add_pipeline_metadata(df, pipeline_name, source_path=None):
    """Add standard metadata columns to a DataFrame before writing.
    
    These columns enable lineage tracing from Gold back to Bronze.
    """
    df = (df
        .withColumn('_processed_at', current_timestamp())
        .withColumn('_pipeline_name', lit(pipeline_name))
        .withColumn('_pipeline_version', lit(
            importlib.metadata.version('nordgrid-pipelines')  # from pyproject.toml
        ))
    )
    
    # If reading from files, capture the source file path
    if source_path:
        df = df.withColumn('_source_file', input_file_name())
    
    return df

# Usage in the Silver pipeline
df_silver = transform_bronze_to_silver(df_bronze)
df_silver = add_pipeline_metadata(df_silver, 'nordgrid-bronze-to-silver', source_path=True)

# Now every Silver row carries:
# _processed_at:      2026-03-22 06:15:23
# _pipeline_name:     nordgrid-bronze-to-silver
# _pipeline_version:  2.4.1
# _source_file:       s3://nordgrid-prod/bronze/readings/date=2026-03-22/part-00003.parquet
Lineage Queries

With metadata columns in every table, lineage queries become straightforward SQL. To find which pipeline version produced a specific Gold row, query the Gold table's _pipeline_version column. To find when a Silver partition was last processed, query the max(_processed_at) for that partition. To trace a Gold number back to Bronze, join the Gold table's metadata with the Silver table's metadata on _processed_at range and _pipeline_name. NordGrid's analytics team uses these lineage queries during quarterly audits to demonstrate that every reported number can be traced to specific source files processed at specific times by specific pipeline versions.

# Lineage query: when was NORTH March data last processed, and by which pipeline version?
spark.sql("""
    SELECT region, date, 
           MAX(_processed_at) AS last_processed,
           MAX(_pipeline_version) AS pipeline_version,
           COUNT(*) AS row_count
    FROM silver.readings_delta
    WHERE region = 'NORTH' AND date BETWEEN '2026-03-01' AND '2026-03-31'
    GROUP BY region, date
    ORDER BY date
""").show(5, truncate=False)

The metadata overhead is minimal: four columns (a timestamp, two short strings, and a file path string) add approximately 100 bytes per row, which is less than 1% of the typical NordGrid row size of 400 bytes. The storage cost across the 3.2TB archive is approximately 30GB — a small price for complete traceability from Gold aggregates back to Bronze source files. NordGrid's metadata convention prefixes all metadata columns with an underscore (_processed_at, _pipeline_name, _pipeline_version) to distinguish them from business data columns. Downstream consumers know to exclude underscore-prefixed columns from aggregations, reports, and dashboard queries.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Add pipeline metadata to every table, at every layer. Bronze tables carry ingestion metadata (ingestion_timestamp, source_file). Silver tables carry transformation metadata (processed_at, pipeline_version, source_file). Gold tables carry aggregation metadata (processed_at, pipeline_version, source_silver_partition). The metadata is added by a shared utility function (add_pipeline_metadata) that every pipeline calls before writing. This consistency ensures that lineage queries work uniformly across the entire platform.

This lesson synthesizes every pattern from the module into NordGrid's complete pipeline architecture at 1.2M-meter scale. The architecture is the product of 18 months of production operation: it started as a simple read-transform-write script (the junior-level pattern) and evolved through each production incident into the resilient, observable system described here. Every pattern in this module was added in response to a real failure: idempotency after the duplicate-data incident, incremental processing after the 35-minute-pipeline SLA breach, late-data handling after the missing-readings discovery, quality gates after the all-null billing event, and metadata after the audit traceability requirement. The architecture is not complex for the sake of complexity — every component earns its place by preventing a specific category of failure.
The Architecture Diagram

blocks.title: NordGrid daily pipeline architecture
blocks.content:
Bronze (Delta)                Silver (Delta)              Gold (Delta)
──────────────                ──────────────              ────────────

┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐
│ CSV/JSON   │ → │ Bronze     │ → │ Silver   │ → │ Gold     │
│ from source│     │ ingestion  │     │ transform│     │ aggregate│
└────────────┘     └──────┬─────┘     └────┬─────┘     └────┬─────┘
                          │                 │                 │
                    ┌─────┴─────┐     ┌────┴─────┐     ┌────┴─────┐
                    │ Checkpoint │     │ Quality  │     │ Checkpoint │
                    │ + HWM      │     │ Gate     │     │ + Metadata │
                    └───────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘

Cross-cutting: Late-data handler (reprocesses last 7 days)
               SCD Type 2 MERGE (meter metadata daily)
               Config from YAML (paths, thresholds, settings)
blocks.caption: Every stage is idempotent (replaceWhere or MERGE). Checkpoints track completion. Quality gates block bad data. Metadata enables lineage. Late-data handler reprocesses affected partitions.

blocks.content: Operational Runbook Summary

rows: Re-run from the beginning — checkpoints skip completed stages
Second run is a no-op for completed stages, processes remaining
Late-data handler reprocesses affected Silver partition
Pipeline bug fixed, last 30 days need regeneration
Gold write blocked, Slack alert, on-call investigates
Auditor asks to reproduce January's report
Query Gold table with timestampAsOf January 31
New region added to the grid
Add region to YAML config, no code change

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Modules 4–5 complete the architecture phase of the mid-level track. You now have both the storage architecture (Delta Lake with schema evolution, MERGE, and time travel) and the pipeline architecture (idempotency, incremental processing, late-data handling, quality gates, and metadata). The next phase — engineering discipline — addresses how to validate these architectures before they reach production. Module 6 covers testing PySpark pipelines: unit tests for transformations, integration tests for the full pipeline, schema contract tests, and quality gate validation with synthetic data. Module 7 covers deployment and CI/CD: automating the path from code change to production with linting, testing, artifact packaging, and staged rollouts.

blocks.title: Module 5 complete
blocks.content: You can now design idempotent pipelines with overwrite, replaceWhere, and MERGE strategies. You can implement incremental processing with high-water marks, handle late-arriving data with reprocessing windows, parameterize pipelines for backfills, track slowly changing dimensions with SCD Type 1 and Type 2 patterns, recover from multi-stage failures with completion markers, externalize configuration into YAML, validate data quality between stages, and embed pipeline metadata for lineage tracing. NordGrid's pipeline architecture diagram and operational runbook provide the blueprint for applying these patterns in production.

chapters.title: Testing PySpark Pipelines
chapters.description: Build a comprehensive testing strategy for PySpark code: unit tests for transformations, integration tests for pipelines, data validation tests for output quality, and the test infrastructure that makes testing practical.

blocks.content: NordGrid's Silver pipeline had a filter condition: col('daily_kw') > 0. During a routine code cleanup, an engineer changed it to col('daily_kw') >= 0 to include zero-value readings from offline meters. The change was reviewed, approved, and deployed. Two weeks later, the billing team noticed that commercial meters were underrepresented in the monthly report by 12%. The investigation traced the problem to a side effect of the filter change: a separate filter downstream used col('customer_type') != 'commercial', but a typo had it as col('customer_type') != 'commerical' (missing an 'r'). With the old > 0 filter, the typo was harmless because the misspelled string never matched anything — all customer types passed. With the new >= 0 filter, zero-value readings had null customer_type, and the != comparison with null produced null (not true), so null-customer-type rows were excluded. The billing loss was $340,000 over two weeks. A unit test on the filter function with a null customer_type row would have caught this in seconds.
The Three Testing Failures

PySpark pipelines fail in three ways that tests can catch. Silent data corruption: the pipeline runs successfully, produces output, passes schema validation, but the numbers are wrong. The filter-typo incident is an example — the output looked plausible, the row count was within the expected range, and nobody noticed until the billing audit. Schema drift: a source system changes its schema, the pipeline ingests data with missing or mistyped columns, and downstream transformations produce nulls or wrong casts. A schema contract test catches this on the first affected run. Aggregation errors: a join produces duplicate keys, an aggregation double-counts rows, or a groupBy uses the wrong column. These errors produce results that are exactly 2x or 0.5x the correct value — close enough to look plausible, wrong enough to cause business damage. Integration tests with known input and expected output catch these errors definitively.
Why PySpark Testing Is Different

Testing PySpark code is harder than testing regular Python code for three reasons. First, PySpark requires a SparkSession, which takes 5–15 seconds to start. If every test starts a new session, a test suite of 50 tests takes 4–12 minutes just on session startup. The solution is a shared session (lesson 3). Second, PySpark operates on DataFrames, not on simple Python values. Asserting that two DataFrames are equal requires comparing schemas, row counts, and cell values — a more complex assertion than assertEqual(a, b). The solution is PySpark's testing utilities (lesson 2). Third, PySpark pipelines read from and write to external storage, which tests must mock or replace with temporary directories. The solution is test data strategies (lesson 4) and temporary output directories (lesson 5). This module addresses all three challenges.
The Testing Pyramid for Data Pipelines

NordGrid's testing strategy follows a pyramid: many unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and a small number of end-to-end quality validation tests at the top. Unit tests verify individual transformation functions (does the tier classification produce the correct tier for a given daily_kw value?). Integration tests verify the complete pipeline on small, representative datasets (does the Bronze-to-Silver pipeline produce the expected Silver schema with the expected row count?). Quality validation tests verify that the output meets business rules (are null rates below the threshold? are all regions represented?). Each layer catches different bugs and has different execution costs. The pyramid ensures comprehensive coverage without excessive test suite duration.

rows: Logic bugs, null handling, edge cases
Complete pipeline: Bronze → Silver → Gold on sample data
Schema mismatches, join errors, aggregation bugs
Output quality: null rates, row counts, value ranges
Data corruption, upstream changes, business rule violations

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's testing ROI calculation after the first year: the team wrote 180 unit tests, 15 integration tests, and 30 quality checks across 6 pipelines. The total development cost was approximately 3 engineer-weeks. In the same year, the tests caught 4 bugs that would have reached production: the filter-null interaction ($340K billing impact), a schema change from a partner (would have corrupted 14 days of data), a join duplication from a reference table update (would have doubled Gold metrics), and a date parsing bug in the backfill pipeline (would have overwritten 30 days of correct data with incorrect data). The prevented cost exceeded $500K. Testing is not overhead — it is insurance.

A unit test for a PySpark transformation follows a three-step pattern: create a small input DataFrame with known values, apply the transformation function, and assert that the output matches expectations.
