Data recovery took 12 minutes (restore today's Silver partition from the previous version, re-run Gold). Total incident duration: 22 minutes. Post-mortem action: the team added a CI plan verification test that checks the daily_kw column type in the physical plan, and changed the deployment strategy for type-affecting changes from direct to blue-green.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's rollback readiness checklist: the last three artifact versions are stored on the deployment server, the rollback command is pre-written in the runbook (not constructed during the incident), the Delta table recovery commands are pre-written for every Silver and Gold table, and every on-call engineer has practiced the rollback procedure during the quarterly incident drill. The drill takes 30 minutes and ensures that the rollback path works before it is needed. Preparation is the difference between a 15-minute incident and a 4-hour incident.

This lesson synthesizes the module into NordGrid's deployment lifecycle: the complete path from a code change to production, the decision points along the way, and the safety nets at each stage. The playbook is not a theoretical ideal — it is the actual process the team follows for every pipeline change, refined through 14 months of production operation and 6 deployment incidents. Each step exists because its absence caused a specific problem. The playbook is printed on a laminated card next to every engineer's monitor and is reviewed during onboarding.
The Deployment Lifecycle

blocks.title: NordGrid deployment lifecycle
blocks.content:
Code Change → PR Review → CI Validation → Staging Deploy → Staging Validate → Prod Deploy → Monitor
     │           │            │                │                │               │           │
     │        Checklist:    Lint, type,     Run pipeline    Compare vs     Deploy      Quality
     │        - tests?      unit tests,     on staging      known-good     strategy    dashboard,
     │        - schema?     config check,   data with       reference      based on    Spark UI,
     │        - deploy      integration,    production      output.        risk level. Slack
     │          strategy?   schema check,   thresholds.     Match within                alerts.
     │                      artifact build.                 tolerance?
     │
  If any gate fails: STOP. Fix before proceeding.
  If production monitoring detects issues: ROLLBACK (code) + RECOVER (data).
blocks.caption: Every stage is a gate: if it fails, the process stops. No stage is optional. The playbook ensures that only validated, staged, and monitored code reaches production.

blocks.content: The Deployment Checklist

rows: Code change with tests, golden schema update if needed
Reviewer approves. CI passes lint + unit + config.
Merge to main triggers full CI
Integration tests pass. Schema contracts pass. Artifact built.
Deploy artifact to staging, run on staging data
Pipeline completes. Output matches golden reference within tolerance.
PR author classifies the change risk
Direct / blue-green / canary strategy selected and documented.
Watch quality dashboard and Spark UI for 24 hours
Quality metrics stable. No SLA breaches. No anomalous shuffle or task durations.
Mark deployment as complete in the change log
All monitoring clear. Deployment record updated.

blocks.content: The Post-Mortem Process

When a deployment causes a production incident, NordGrid follows a blameless post-mortem process. The post-mortem document answers five questions: what happened (timeline of events), what was the impact (data affected, duration, downstream effects), why did it happen (root cause analysis tracing back to the code change and the gap in the validation process), how was it resolved (rollback and recovery steps), and what will prevent recurrence (specific actions: new test, new CI gate, changed deployment strategy, updated runbook). The post-mortem is written within 48 hours, reviewed by the team, and the action items are tracked to completion. NordGrid has completed 6 post-mortems in 14 months, and each one added at least one new CI gate or testing requirement.
What Comes Next

Modules 6–7 complete the engineering discipline phase. You can now test PySpark pipelines comprehensively (Module 6) and deploy them safely through a CI/CD pipeline with staging validation, risk-based deployment strategies, and pre-built rollback procedures (this module). The next phase — operations — covers the skills needed to keep deployed pipelines running reliably: memory management and caching (Module 8), performance monitoring and tuning (Module 9), and structured streaming (Module 10). Where this module covered how to put code into production, the operations modules cover how to keep it there.

blocks.title: Module 7 complete
blocks.content: You can now transition notebooks to production scripts with proper structure, parameterization, and error handling. You can automate spark-submit from YAML configuration, orchestrate multi-stage pipelines with Airflow, manage environment-specific configurations safely, package and distribute Python and Java dependencies, build CI pipelines that validate code, plans, schemas, and data quality, choose deployment strategies based on change risk, and roll back code and recover data when deployments go wrong. NordGrid's deployment playbook — the lifecycle diagram, the checklist, and the post-mortem process — provides the operational framework for safe, repeatable deployment of PySpark pipelines.

chapters.title: Memory Management and Caching
chapters.description: Understand Spark’s memory architecture, diagnose out-of-memory errors, design effective caching strategies, and configure memory settings that balance performance and stability.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, memory was invisible: you wrote transformations and Spark managed the memory. At mid-level scale — 1.2 million meters, 3.2TB of historical data, and pipelines that join, aggregate, and cache large DataFrames — memory becomes a resource you must understand and manage. When a pipeline fails with OutOfMemoryError, you need to know whether the problem is in execution memory (a shuffle partition too large to sort), storage memory (too much data cached), user memory (a Python UDF accumulating state), or driver memory (a collect() that pulled too many rows). This module teaches you Spark's memory model from the inside out, starting with the unified memory architecture that governs how every byte is allocated.
The Unified Memory Model

Each Spark executor has a fixed amount of JVM heap memory, set by spark.executor.memory. Spark divides this heap into three regions. The Spark memory region (default 60% of the heap) is split between execution memory (for shuffles, joins, sorts, and aggregations) and storage memory (for cached DataFrames and broadcast variables). The user memory region (default 40% of the remaining heap after Spark memory and reserved) is for your own data structures: Python UDF state, accumulators, and any objects your code creates. The reserved region (300MB) is set aside for Spark's internal bookkeeping. The key insight is that execution and storage share a pool: when execution needs more memory than its half allows, it can borrow from storage (evicting cached data), and vice versa. This dynamic sharing is why Spark's memory model is called 'unified.'

blocks.title: Executor JVM heap memory layout
blocks.content:
spark.executor.memory = 4g

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         JVM Heap (4096 MB)                        │
├──────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┬────────┤
│   Reserved   │          Spark Memory (60%)              │  User  │
│   (300 MB)   ├─────────────────┬──────────────────┤ Memory │
│              │  Execution       │  Storage          │  (40%) │
│              │  (shuffles,      │  (cache,          │        │
│              │   joins, sorts)  │   broadcast)      │        │
│              │  ~1140 MB        │  ~1140 MB         │~1516MB│
│              │       ←─── can borrow ───►       │        │
└──────────────┴─────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────┘

spark.memory.fraction = 0.6       (Spark memory = 60% of heap after reserved)
spark.memory.storageFraction = 0.5 (storage gets 50% of Spark memory initially)
blocks.caption: Execution and storage share the Spark memory pool. When execution needs more space, it can evict cached data from storage. When storage is idle, execution can use the entire pool.

blocks.content: The Borrowing Mechanism

The borrowing between execution and storage follows an asymmetric rule. Execution can evict storage at any time: if a shuffle needs 1500MB and execution's half is only 1140MB, it evicts 360MB of cached data from storage to make room. Storage can only borrow from execution when execution is not using the space: if no shuffle is in progress, storage can use the full 2280MB Spark memory pool for caching. But once execution reclaims its space (a new shuffle starts), storage must release the borrowed memory — which means evicting cached data. This asymmetry exists because execution is more time-sensitive: a shuffle that runs out of memory spills to disk or fails, while evicted cache can be recomputed. The practical consequence: caching is opportunistic, not guaranteed. Do not assume cached data will stay in memory if your pipeline has large shuffles.
Configuration Properties

# The three memory configuration properties
# Usually, the defaults are correct. Change only after measurement.

spark.conf.set('spark.executor.memory', '4g')          # total JVM heap per executor
spark.conf.set('spark.memory.fraction', '0.6')          # % of heap (after reserved) for Spark
spark.conf.set('spark.memory.storageFraction', '0.5')   # % of Spark memory reserved for storage

# NordGrid production defaults:
# Daily pipeline:      executor.memory = 4g  (handles 200MB partitions comfortably)
# Historical reprocess: executor.memory = 8g  (handles 2GB partitions with headroom)
# Analytical notebooks: executor.memory = 8g  (caching + interactive queries)

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's memory rule: do not change spark.memory.fraction or spark.memory.storageFraction from their defaults (0.6 and 0.5). These have been tuned across millions of Spark applications and work well for the vast majority of workloads. Instead, adjust spark.executor.memory to give executors more or less total heap. The partition count (Module 2, lesson 4) determines how much data each task processes, which determines how much execution memory each task needs. Increasing partition count reduces per-task memory pressure more effectively than increasing executor memory.

The previous lesson covered the JVM heap. But an executor's total memory footprint extends beyond the heap. The YARN or Kubernetes resource manager allocates a container for each executor, and that container must account for the JVM heap, the JVM's off-heap memory (metaspace, thread stacks, NIO buffers), and the overhead for PySpark's Python worker processes. If the container is too small, the resource manager kills the executor for exceeding its memory limit — a different failure mode than the JVM's OutOfMemoryError. Understanding the full memory layout prevents both JVM OOM (heap too small) and container kills (total memory exceeds the YARN allocation).
The Full Executor Memory Breakdown

blocks.title: Executor container memory breakdown
blocks.caption: The YARN container must be large enough for both the JVM heap and the overhead. If PySpark UDFs consume significant off-heap memory, the default overhead may not be sufficient.

blocks.content: The PySpark Overhead Problem

PySpark adds a complication that pure Scala Spark does not have: Python worker processes. When a PySpark UDF executes, Spark spawns a Python process alongside the JVM on each executor. The Python process has its own memory footprint (the Python interpreter, imported libraries, and the data being processed). This memory is not part of the JVM heap — it is allocated from the container's overhead region. NordGrid experienced this when a pipeline added a Python UDF that loaded a 200MB lookup dictionary into memory on each executor. The JVM heap had plenty of room, but the Python workers consumed the entire overhead allocation, and YARN killed the executors for exceeding the container memory limit. The error message said 'Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits' — not a JVM OutOfMemoryError. The fix was increasing spark.executor.memoryOverhead from the default 512MB to 1g.

# Memory configuration for PySpark pipelines with UDFs
spark.conf.set('spark.executor.memory', '4g')              # JVM heap
spark.conf.set('spark.executor.memoryOverhead', '1g')      # off-heap (Python workers, NIO)
# Total YARN container: 4g + 1g = 5g

# For pipelines WITHOUT UDFs (pure DataFrame API), the default overhead is sufficient
spark.conf.set('spark.executor.memory', '4g')
# spark.executor.memoryOverhead defaults to max(384MB, 10% of 4g) = 410MB
# Total YARN container: 4g + 410MB = ~4.4g

# NordGrid convention:
# Pipelines with UDFs:    memoryOverhead = 1g (or 25% of executor.memory)
# Pipelines without UDFs: memoryOverhead = default (10%)

blocks.title: Container kill vs JVM OOM
blocks.content: Two different failure modes look similar but have different root causes. JVM OutOfMemoryError means the JVM heap is exhausted — increase spark.executor.memory or reduce partition size. 'Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits' means the total process memory (JVM heap + off-heap) exceeds the YARN container — increase spark.executor.memoryOverhead. NordGrid's on-call runbook checks the error message first to determine which knob to adjust. Increasing the wrong one wastes resources without fixing the problem.

An OutOfMemoryError tells you that something ran out of memory. It does not tell you what, where, or why. The diagnostic challenge is that OOM errors can originate from five different sources in a PySpark pipeline: execution memory during a shuffle or sort, storage memory from excessive caching, user memory from Python UDFs or accumulators, driver memory from collect() or broadcast(), and container overhead from Python worker processes. Each source has a different fix, and applying the wrong fix wastes time and resources. This lesson provides the diagnostic workflow NordGrid's on-call engineers use to identify the OOM source in under 5 minutes.
The OOM Diagnostic Workflow

rows: Error log mentions 'driver' or the Spark UI is inaccessible
The driver ran out of heap. Likely cause: collect(), toPandas(), or broadcast() brought too much data to the driver. See lesson 9.
Did an EXECUTOR get killed by YARN/K8s?
Error says 'Container killed for exceeding memory limits'
Total memory (heap + overhead) exceeded the container. Likely cause: PySpark UDF memory or excessive off-heap buffers. Increase memoryOverhead.
Did an executor throw JVM OOM during a shuffle?
Error occurs in a stage with Exchange operator. Spark UI shows spill > 0.
Shuffle partitions too large. Increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to reduce per-partition size.
Did an executor throw JVM OOM during a broadcast?
Error mentions 'broadcast' or occurs on a BroadcastHashJoin stage
Broadcast table too large for executor memory. Remove broadcast hint, reduce threshold, or pre-filter the broadcast table.
Did an executor throw JVM OOM during caching?
Spark UI Storage tab shows high cache usage. Error during persist().
Too much data cached. Unpersist DataFrames no longer needed. Consider MEMORY_AND_DISK instead of MEMORY_ONLY.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Three Most Common OOM Scenarios

Scenario 1: Shuffle OOM on the historical reprocessing job. The job processes 3.2TB with spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 200, producing 16GB partitions. Executors with 4GB heap cannot sort a 16GB partition. Fix: increase shuffle partitions to 1,600 (2GB per partition). Scenario 2: Driver OOM from a monitoring query. An analyst wrote df.toPandas() on the full Silver table to visualize in matplotlib. The driver tried to collect 1.2 million rows into driver memory. Fix: add .limit(10000) before toPandas(), or use .sample(fraction=0.01). Scenario 3: Container kill from a geocoding UDF. A Python UDF loaded a 300MB reverse-geocoding database on each executor. The default 410MB overhead could not hold the database plus the Python interpreter. Fix: increase memoryOverhead to 1g.
Reading OOM Error Messages

# Executor JVM OOM during shuffle
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
  at org.apache.spark.util.collection.unsafe.sort.UnsafeExternalSorter.spill
  at org.apache.spark.sql.execution.UnsafeExternalRowSorter
→ The sorter ran out of heap during the sort phase of a shuffle.
→ Fix: increase partition count or executor memory.

# Driver OOM from collect()
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
  at org.apache.spark.sql.execution.SparkPlan.executeCollect
→ The driver tried to collect too much data.
→ Fix: add .limit() or .sample() before collect/toPandas.

# YARN container kill
ExecutorLostFailure: Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits.
Considered 5120 MB of 4608 MB physical memory used.
→ Total process memory (5120MB) exceeded the container limit (4608MB).
→ Fix: increase spark.executor.memoryOverhead.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's OOM response time target: identify the source within 5 minutes using the diagnostic table, apply the fix, and re-run. The on-call engineer does not guess — they check the error message and the Spark UI metrics in order. The fix for shuffle OOM is always 'increase partition count' (not 'increase memory'), because increasing partition count is a configuration change while increasing memory requires cluster scaling. NordGrid only increases executor memory when partition count tuning is insufficient.

Caching stores a DataFrame's computed results in executor memory (or on disk) so that subsequent actions on the same DataFrame do not recompute it from scratch. Without caching, every action triggers a full recomputation from the data source. This is fine when the DataFrame is used once, but expensive when the DataFrame is used multiple times. NordGrid's Silver enrichment pipeline reads 1.2 million meter readings from Parquet, joins them with three reference tables, and then runs two separate aggregations on the enriched result: one for the regional summary and one for the billing detail. Without caching, the read-and-join sequence executes twice — once for each aggregation. With caching, the enriched DataFrame is computed once, stored in memory, and reused for both aggregations, cutting the total pipeline duration by 40%.
The Caching Decision

Caching is beneficial when three conditions are met simultaneously: the DataFrame is expensive to compute (it involves reads, joins, or complex transformations), the DataFrame is used by multiple downstream actions (two or more actions reference it), and the DataFrame fits in executor memory without evicting execution memory needed for shuffles. If any condition is not met, caching either provides no benefit or actively degrades performance. Caching a DataFrame that is used only once wastes memory without saving any computation. Caching a DataFrame that is cheap to recompute (a small lookup table read from local Parquet) wastes memory for negligible time savings. Caching a DataFrame that is larger than the available storage memory forces eviction and may cause spill in concurrent operations.

rows: DataFrame used twice (read → join → agg1 + agg2)
The join is expensive. Caching avoids recomputing it for the second aggregation.
Small lookup table (200 rows, read from Parquet)
Recomputation is instant. Caching adds overhead for no benefit.
Large intermediate result (1.2M rows) used 3 times
Three recomputations of the join would triple the pipeline duration.
DataFrame larger than available storage memory
Cache with MEMORY_AND_DISK. Pure MEMORY_ONLY will evict other cached data or spill.

blocks.content: Measuring the Impact

import time

df_enriched = (spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings/')
    .join(broadcast(substations), 'substation_id', 'left')
    .join(meters, 'meter_id', 'left')
    .join(broadcast(weather), ['region', 'date'], 'left')
)

# WITHOUT cache: both aggregations recompute the join
start = time.time()
df_summary = df_enriched.groupBy('region').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
df_summary.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('gold/summary/')
df_billing = df_enriched.groupBy('meter_id', 'month').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
df_billing.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('gold/billing/')
print(f"Without cache: {time.time() - start:.0f}s")

# WITH cache: join is computed once and reused
start = time.time()
df_enriched.cache()    # mark for caching
df_enriched.count()    # trigger computation and caching
df_summary = df_enriched.groupBy('region').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
df_summary.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('gold/summary/')
df_billing = df_enriched.groupBy('meter_id', 'month').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
df_billing.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('gold/billing/')
df_enriched.unpersist()  # release memory after final use
print(f"With cache: {time.time() - start:.0f}s")

# NordGrid result: Without cache: 270s. With cache: 165s. (40% reduction)

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's caching rule: cache a DataFrame only when it is used by two or more downstream actions AND the recomputation cost exceeds 30 seconds. Measure first, cache second. After adding a cache, verify in the Spark UI's Storage tab that the DataFrame is actually cached (it may be too large and get evicted immediately). And always unpersist after the final use — a forgotten cache is memory that other operations cannot use.

When you call df.cache(), Spark stores the DataFrame using the default storage level: MEMORY_AND_DISK. This means Spark tries to keep the data in memory, and if executor memory runs out, it spills partitions to the executor's local disk. But the default is not always the best choice. For small reference tables that fit comfortably in memory, MEMORY_ONLY avoids the overhead of disk I/O. For large intermediate results that exceed available memory, MEMORY_AND_DISK is correct but you should verify that disk spill is not degrading performance. For situations where memory is scarce and disk I/O is acceptable, DISK_ONLY frees memory entirely. And for very large DataFrames where memory is tight, MEMORY_ONLY_SER stores data in a compact serialized format that uses 2–5x less memory at the cost of CPU for deserialization. Understanding these options lets you match the storage strategy to the data size and memory constraints.
The Storage Levels

rows: Memory, spills to disk if needed
High in memory, disk for overflow
Lost partitions are recomputed or read from disk
Serialized in memory, spills to disk
Low in memory, disk for overflow
Lost partitions recomputed or read from disk

blocks.content: Choosing the Right Level

from pyspark import StorageLevel

# Small reference table (< 100MB): MEMORY_ONLY — fast reads, no disk overhead
df_substations.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY)

# Large intermediate result (1–2GB): MEMORY_AND_DISK — default, handles overflow
df_enriched.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)  # equivalent to df.cache()

# Very large result (>2GB) with tight memory: MEMORY_ONLY_SER — compact format
df_archive.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY_SER)

# Memory-constrained cluster: DISK_ONLY — frees memory entirely
df_huge.persist(StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY)

NordGrid's caching policy maps storage levels to table types. Small reference tables (substations, weather, region config) use MEMORY_ONLY because they fit in memory on every executor and are read thousands of times during broadcasts and lookups. The enriched Silver DataFrame (1.2M rows, ~3GB deserialized) uses MEMORY_AND_DISK because it typically fits in memory but may spill during periods of high cluster utilization. The historical archive (3.2TB) is never cached — it is too large for any caching strategy to be cost-effective, and the pipeline processes it incrementally (one day at a time) so recomputation cost is bounded.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's storage level decision tree: if the DataFrame is under 200MB, use MEMORY_ONLY. If it is 200MB–2GB and the cluster has memory headroom, use MEMORY_AND_DISK (default). If it is 200MB–2GB but the cluster is memory-constrained (other pipelines running concurrently), use MEMORY_ONLY_SER for 2–5x memory reduction. If it is over 2GB, do not cache — redesign the pipeline to reduce recomputation through checkpointing or breaking the pipeline into stages that write intermediate results.

Caching is not set-and-forget. A cached DataFrame occupies memory from the moment it is materialized until it is explicitly unpersisted or the SparkSession ends. Forgetting to unpersist a DataFrame after its final use is one of the most common memory management mistakes in PySpark pipelines. The cached data competes with execution memory for shuffles and joins in subsequent stages, increasing the likelihood of spill or OOM. NordGrid's pipeline that caches the enriched DataFrame for two Gold aggregations originally forgot to unpersist it. The subsequent late-data handler's shuffle spilled to disk because the cached data occupied 1.5GB of storage memory that the shuffle could not reclaim fast enough. Adding a single line — df_enriched.unpersist() — after the second aggregation eliminated the spill entirely.
The Cache Lifecycle Pattern

# The correct cache lifecycle: persist, use, unpersist

# Step 1: Build the expensive DataFrame
df_enriched = build_enriched_readings(spark, config)

# Step 2: Persist (mark for caching)
df_enriched.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)

# Step 3: Trigger materialization (the cache is populated by the first action)
df_enriched.count()  # or any action

# Step 4: Use the cached DataFrame for multiple downstream operations
df_summary = df_enriched.groupBy('region').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
df_summary.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save(config['gold_summary_path'])

df_billing = df_enriched.groupBy('meter_id', 'month').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
df_billing.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save(config['gold_billing_path'])

# Step 5: Unpersist immediately after the final use
df_enriched.unpersist()  # releases memory for subsequent operations

# Step 6: Subsequent operations now have full memory available
run_late_data_handler(spark, config)  # no longer competes with the cache
Monitoring Cache in the Spark UI

The Spark UI's Storage tab shows every cached DataFrame: its storage level, the number of partitions cached, the fraction cached (some partitions may have been evicted), and the total size in memory and on disk. NordGrid's on-call engineers check this tab when investigating memory-related performance issues. A common finding: a DataFrame that was cached during an early stage is still cached during a later stage where it is no longer needed, consuming memory that the later stage's shuffle requires. The Storage tab makes this visible: if you see a cached DataFrame that your pipeline is not currently using, it should be unpersisted.

The Storage tab also reveals partial caching: when a DataFrame is too large to fit entirely in memory with MEMORY_ONLY, some partitions are cached and others are not. The 'Fraction Cached' column shows the percentage. If only 30% of partitions are cached, the recomputation savings are limited because 70% of the data must be recomputed anyway. In this case, switching to MEMORY_AND_DISK caches all partitions (overflow goes to disk), or switching to MEMORY_ONLY_SER reduces the memory footprint enough to cache a higher fraction in memory.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's caching discipline: every persist() call has a corresponding unpersist() call after the final use. The two calls are in the same function or the same pipeline stage, never separated across stages where the unpersist might be forgotten. The pipeline template includes a comment pattern: '# CACHE START: df_enriched' and '# CACHE END: df_enriched.unpersist()' to make the lifecycle visible during code review. The code review checklist includes: 'For every persist(), is there a matching unpersist() after the final use?'

Module 3, lesson 2 covered broadcast joins where a small DataFrame is sent to every executor for a shuffle-free join. Broadcast variables extend this concept beyond joins: you can broadcast any read-only data structure — a Python dictionary, a set, a list, a configuration object — to every executor. The broadcast is sent once, stored in each executor's memory, and accessible by every task on that executor without network transfer. NordGrid uses broadcast variables for lookup data that is too complex for a simple join: the 12-region configuration map (pricing tiers, timezone offsets, regulatory codes), the valid meter ID set for fast membership checks in UDFs, and the tiered pricing schedule that changes quarterly.
The sc.broadcast() API

# Create a broadcast variable from a Python dictionary
region_config = {
    'NORTH': {'timezone': 'Europe/Stockholm', 'base_rate': 0.12, 'peak_hours': (7, 21)},
    'SOUTH': {'timezone': 'Europe/Stockholm', 'base_rate': 0.10, 'peak_hours': (8, 20)},
    'EAST':  {'timezone': 'Europe/Helsinki', 'base_rate': 0.11, 'peak_hours': (7, 21)},
    # ... 9 more regions
}

# Broadcast the dictionary to all executors
bc_region_config = spark.sparkContext.broadcast(region_config)

# Use inside a UDF: access with .value
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType

@udf(returnType=DoubleType())
def calculate_rate(region, hour):
    config = bc_region_config.value  # read from executor-local copy, no network
    if region in config:
        rc = config[region]
        peak_start, peak_end = rc['peak_hours']
        return rc['base_rate'] * (1.5 if peak_start <= hour < peak_end else 1.0)
    return 0.0

df = df.withColumn('rate', calculate_rate(col('region'), col('hour')))
Broadcast Variables vs Broadcast Joins

Broadcast joins (Module 3) and broadcast variables serve different purposes. A broadcast join sends a small DataFrame to every executor for a shuffle-free equi-join on a key column. A broadcast variable sends any Python object to every executor for arbitrary lookup logic. Use broadcast joins when the relationship between the two datasets is a key-based match (readings.substation_id = substations.substation_id). Use broadcast variables when the lookup logic is more complex: range-based lookups (pricing tiers based on kW ranges), multi-attribute lookups (region + hour + customer_type), or set membership checks (is this meter_id in the active fleet set?). Broadcast variables are typically used inside UDFs, while broadcast joins are pure DataFrame operations.
Memory Implications

A broadcast variable is stored in each executor's memory for the duration of its lifecycle. A 10MB dictionary broadcast to 8 executors consumes 80MB of cluster memory total (10MB per executor). NordGrid's region config is 50KB — negligible. But the valid meter ID set is 25MB (1.2 million strings), and the pricing schedule with historical versions is 100MB. Both are broadcast at the start of the pipeline and remain in memory until the SparkContext is stopped or the variable is explicitly destroyed with bc_var.destroy(). If a pipeline broadcasts multiple large variables, the cumulative memory impact can be significant. NordGrid's convention: broadcast variables under 50MB are fine. Variables between 50MB and 200MB require a comment justifying the broadcast. Variables over 200MB should use a broadcast join or a different approach.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Prefer broadcast joins over broadcast variables when possible, because broadcast joins are pure DataFrame operations that Catalyst can optimize (pushdown, pruning), while broadcast variables inside UDFs are opaque to the optimizer. NordGrid uses broadcast variables only when the lookup logic cannot be expressed as a join: range-based pricing, complex conditional logic, or set membership checks that are faster with a Python set than with an anti-join. The broadcast variable is created once at pipeline startup and destroyed in the finally block alongside spark.stop().

When a task's working data exceeds its available execution memory, Spark does not immediately fail. Instead, it spills the excess data to the executor's local disk: the data is serialized, written to a temporary file, and read back when needed. Spill is a safety mechanism that prevents OOM for moderately oversized partitions, but it comes at a severe performance cost: disk I/O is 10–100x slower than memory access, and the serialization/deserialization adds CPU overhead. A sort-merge join that processes a 500MB partition in 3 seconds with no spill may take 30 seconds with 200MB of spill because the sort repeatedly reads and writes to disk. Detecting and reducing spill is one of the highest-impact performance optimizations for join and aggregation stages.
Detecting Spill in the Spark UI

The Spark UI's Stages tab shows two spill metrics for every stage: 'Spill (Memory)' is the total amount of data that was spilled (before serialization), and 'Spill (Disk)' is the amount of data actually written to disk (after serialization and compression). Non-zero values in either column indicate that at least one task in the stage exceeded its memory allocation. The Summary Metrics table shows percentiles: if the 75th percentile has zero spill but the max has 500MB, one or a few tasks spilled heavily — likely a data skew problem (Module 3). If the median has 100MB of spill, most tasks are too large — likely a partition count problem (Module 2).
Three Approaches to Reduce Spill

rows: Smaller partitions need less memory per task
Increased shuffle partitions from 48 to 96 for the aggregation stage
More memory available per task before spill
Higher cluster cost, fewer executors per node
Increased from 4g to 8g for the historical reprocessing job
Less data per partition before the operation
Requires code changes to add explicit filters/selects
Added date filter before the join to reduce input by 99.7%

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Spill Tuning

# NordGrid's spill monitoring query (run after pipeline execution)
def check_spill_metrics(spark, app_id=None):
    """Report spill metrics from the most recent application."""
    # The Spark UI REST API provides stage metrics programmatically
    # For manual checking, navigate to Spark UI → Stages → sort by Spill (Disk)
    
    print("Spill Monitoring Checklist:")
    print("  1. Open Spark UI → Stages tab")
    print("  2. Sort by 'Spill (Disk)' descending")
    print("  3. If any stage has Spill (Disk) > 0:")
    print("     a. Check if spill is concentrated in one task (skew) or spread (partition too large)")
    print("     b. If skew: apply AQE or salting (Module 3)")
    print("     c. If evenly spread: increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions")
    print("     d. If already high partition count: increase spark.executor.memory")
    print("  4. NordGrid threshold: Spill (Disk) > 1GB on any stage triggers investigation")

NordGrid monitors spill as a weekly metric. The pipeline metadata table records the maximum spill across all stages for every run. A weekly dashboard shows the spill trend. If spill exceeds 1GB on any stage, the on-call engineer investigates using the diagnostic table above. Most spill incidents are resolved by increasing the partition count — the cheapest fix because it is a configuration change, not a code change and not a cluster scaling decision. NordGrid's daily pipeline operates with zero spill under normal conditions. Spill appears only during the monthly reprocessing job (which processes 30x more data than the daily batch) and is managed by using a higher partition count for that job's configuration.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Zero spill is the target for daily pipelines. Any spill means per-task data exceeds available memory, which is a performance problem waiting to become a stability problem. If data grows 10% and the pipeline was already spilling a little, the 10% growth may push spill from 'slow' to 'OOM.' NordGrid treats non-zero spill on daily pipelines as a warning to tune before it becomes an incident.

Every lesson so far focused on executor memory. But the driver has its own memory limit (spark.driver.memory), and certain operations bring data from the executors to the driver, where it must fit in driver heap. The driver is a single process with no parallelism and no disk spill — if the data exceeds driver memory, the application crashes. The operations that bring data to the driver are well-known, but engineers forget them because the DataFrame API makes them look like any other transformation. collect() returns all rows as a Python list on the driver. toPandas() converts the entire DataFrame to a Pandas DataFrame on the driver. broadcast() sends the collected data from the driver to executors. If any of these operates on a DataFrame with millions of rows, the driver runs out of memory.
The Dangerous Operations

rows: Returns ALL rows to the driver as a Python list
Converts the entire DataFrame to a Pandas DataFrame
High — same as collect() plus Pandas memory
Collects df to driver, then sends to executors
High if df is large after filtering
Verify df size before broadcast. Use broadcast only under 200MB.
show() with a large n collects many rows
Moderate — show(1000000) brings 1M rows to driver
The count itself is tiny, but computing it may collect intermediate data
Low risk for the result, but the operation is expensive

blocks.content: Defensive Patterns

# DANGEROUS: collect the entire Silver table to the driver
all_rows = df.collect()  # 1.2M rows → driver OOM

# SAFE: limit before collect
sample_rows = df.limit(100).collect()  # 100 rows → safe

# DANGEROUS: toPandas on the full DataFrame
pdf = df.toPandas()  # 1.2M rows → driver OOM

# SAFE: aggregate first, then toPandas on the small result
pdf = (df.groupBy('region').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
    .toPandas())  # 12 rows → safe

# SAFE: sample a fraction for visualization
pdf_sample = df.sample(fraction=0.01).toPandas()  # ~12K rows → manageable

# DEFENSIVE: check size before broadcast
row_count = df_small.count()
assert row_count < 500_000, f"Too many rows to broadcast: {row_count:,}"
df_joined = df_large.join(broadcast(df_small), 'key', 'left')
Driver Memory Configuration

NordGrid sets driver memory based on the pipeline type. Production batch pipelines use spark.driver.memory = 4g — the driver needs enough memory for the plan, the broadcast tables, and the configuration state, but it should not hold large datasets. Analytical notebooks use spark.driver.memory = 8g — analysts use toPandas() for visualization and need more headroom for interactive queries that collect aggregated results. The historical reprocessing job uses spark.driver.memory = 4g because it processes data on executors and only collects small metrics (row counts, quality check results) to the driver. Increasing driver memory beyond what the pipeline's driver-bound operations need wastes cluster resources because the driver runs on a single node.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's driver safety rule: never call collect() or toPandas() on a DataFrame without first reducing it to a known small size via limit(), sample(), groupBy().agg(), or filter(). The code review checklist includes: 'For every collect() and toPandas(), verify that the DataFrame has been reduced to a bounded size.' Production pipelines should never need more than 4GB of driver memory — if they do, the pipeline is pulling too much data to the driver and should be redesigned.

This lesson synthesizes the module into NordGrid's memory configuration standards, caching policy, and the memory tuning playbook used during quarterly capacity reviews. The standards are not theoretical guidelines — they are the actual values the team uses in production, calibrated through 14 months of operation and 3 OOM incidents. Each standard has a rationale tied to a specific workload characteristic and a threshold that triggers re-evaluation. The playbook provides the systematic approach for adjusting memory settings when data volume grows, new pipelines are added, or cluster hardware changes.
Memory Configuration Standards

rows: 200MB partitions, no UDFs, no large broadcasts
Small output, aggregation only, no joins on Gold

blocks.content: Caching Policy

rows: Enriched Silver DataFrame (used by 2+ Gold pipelines)
After the last Gold pipeline writes
Write intermediate to Delta instead of caching
Too large for any caching strategy

blocks.content: The Memory Tuning Playbook

NordGrid's quarterly capacity review follows a four-step playbook. Step 1: collect metrics. Pull the last 90 days of pipeline metrics from the metadata table: max spill per stage, max GC time as a percentage of task time, max task duration ratio (for skew), and cache eviction events. Step 2: identify hotspots. Any pipeline where spill exceeds 1GB, GC exceeds 15% of task time, or cache eviction occurs regularly is a candidate for tuning. Step 3: diagnose using the OOM workflow from lesson 3. Determine whether the issue is partition size, executor memory, overhead, or caching policy. Step 4: apply the minimum effective change. Increase partition count before increasing memory. Increase memory before adding executors. Add executors before scaling the cluster. Each step is cheaper and less disruptive than the next.
What Comes Next

This module gave you the memory model, the diagnostic workflow, and the tuning playbook for keeping PySpark pipelines stable under memory pressure. Module 9 builds on this foundation with systematic performance monitoring: instrumenting pipelines with timing and row count metrics, interpreting the Spark UI's Jobs, Stages, and Executors tabs in depth, diagnosing specific bottleneck categories (shuffle-bound, scan-bound, memory-bound), and building the monitoring dashboards and regression detection systems that keep pipelines performing within SLA over time. Where this module focused on memory specifically, Module 9 covers the full spectrum of performance concerns across every stage of the execution plan.

blocks.title: Module 8 complete
blocks.content: You now understand Spark's unified memory model (execution and storage sharing a pool), the full executor memory layout (JVM heap, overhead, Python workers), and the diagnostic workflow for OOM errors (driver vs executor, heap vs container, shuffle vs broadcast vs cache). You can design caching strategies with the right storage level, manage cache lifecycles with explicit unpersist, use broadcast variables for complex lookups, detect and reduce spill, and avoid driver memory traps. NordGrid's memory standards and quarterly tuning playbook provide the operational framework for keeping memory-related issues under control as data volume grows.

chapters.title: Performance Monitoring and Tuning
chapters.description: Build a systematic approach to performance monitoring: instrument pipelines with metrics, interpret Spark UI data, diagnose common performance problems, and apply targeted tuning techniques.

blocks.content: NordGrid's daily Silver pipeline ran in 12 minutes. An engineer noticed and decided to optimize. Without checking the Spark UI, they increased executor memory from 4g to 8g (the pipeline was not memory-bound), added broadcast hints to tables that were already being broadcast (no change), and repartitioned the DataFrame to 128 partitions (the pipeline was already at 48, which was correct for the data volume). After three hours of changes and testing, the pipeline ran in 11 minutes and 40 seconds — a 3% improvement from random adjustments. A senior engineer then spent 5 minutes in the Spark UI, found that the slowest stage was a sort-merge join where one side was 45MB (just below the broadcast threshold), added a single broadcast hint, and the pipeline dropped to 7 minutes. The difference: guesswork versus diagnosis.
The Measure-Diagnose-Fix-Verify Cycle

Performance tuning at mid-level is a disciplined four-step cycle, not a collection of tricks. Step 1 — Measure: establish a baseline by recording the pipeline's current duration, shuffle volume, spill, and task distribution before making any changes. Step 2 — Diagnose: use the Spark UI to identify the single most expensive stage and determine why it is expensive (shuffle-bound, scan-bound, memory-bound, or skew-bound). Step 3 — Fix: apply a targeted change that addresses the diagnosed bottleneck. Step 4 — Verify: re-run the pipeline, compare the metrics against the baseline, and confirm the improvement is real and stable. Skipping any step leads to the guesswork trap: changes that feel productive but do not address the actual bottleneck.
The Three Bottleneck Categories

rows: Shuffle Read/Write is the largest metric in the slowest stage
Too much data entering the shuffle (missing pre-filter or column prune)
Pre-filter and prune before the shuffle (Module 3, lesson 7)
Input Size is the largest metric; FileScan takes most of the stage time
Partition pruning not working, or reading unnecessary columns
Fix partition filters, add explicit select (Module 1, lessons 4–5)
Spill (Disk) > 0, or GC Time > 15% of task time
Partitions too large for executor memory, or excessive caching
Increase partition count, reduce cache, or increase executor memory (Module 8)

blocks.content: Every performance problem falls into one or more of these categories. A pipeline can be shuffle-bound and memory-bound simultaneously (large shuffles that also spill), or scan-bound and shuffle-bound (reading too much data and then shuffling all of it). The diagnostic step identifies which category dominates, and the fix targets that category first. NordGrid's rule: fix the dominant bottleneck first, then re-measure. Do not try to fix all three at once because the interactions are complex and the combined changes are hard to verify. One change, one measurement, one verification. Then repeat if needed.
Baselines and SLAs

You cannot detect degradation without a baseline. NordGrid records four metrics after every pipeline run: total duration (wall clock), total shuffle volume (bytes shuffled across all stages), maximum spill (bytes spilled to disk in the worst stage), and max/median task duration ratio (the skew indicator). These four numbers form the performance baseline. When any metric deviates by more than 2x from the 30-day rolling average, an alert fires. The SLA is defined in terms of duration: the daily Silver pipeline must complete before 7 AM (60-minute SLA from the 6 AM trigger). The baseline metrics help the team stay ahead of the SLA: a gradual increase in shuffle volume indicates growing data that will eventually breach the SLA, even if the current duration is within limits.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's performance rule: never optimize without first identifying the bottleneck in the Spark UI. The Spark UI tells you exactly which stage is slowest, which tasks are outliers, how much data was shuffled, and how much spilled. The bottleneck determines the fix. Applying a fix without a diagnosis is guesswork, and guesswork has a 90% chance of targeting the wrong bottleneck.

sections.title: Spark UI Deep Dive — Jobs and Stages

blocks.content: Module 1, lesson 9 introduced the Spark UI at a conceptual level. This lesson teaches advanced interpretation of the Jobs and Stages tabs — the two tabs you use most often for performance diagnosis. The Jobs tab shows every action (write, count, show) as a job with a duration and a DAG of stages. The Stages tab shows every stage with detailed metrics: input size, output size, shuffle read, shuffle write, spill, GC time, and per-task statistics. Together, these two tabs answer the question 'which stage is the bottleneck and why?' for any pipeline run.
Reading the Jobs Tab

Each action in your pipeline creates one job. A pipeline that reads, transforms, and writes creates one job for the write action (the read and transform are lazy and execute as part of the write's job). A pipeline that writes Silver and then writes Gold creates two jobs. The Jobs tab lists each job with its duration, the number of stages, and a status (succeeded, failed, running). Click a job to see its stage DAG — a visual graph showing the stage sequence and the shuffle boundaries between them. The stage DAG is the fastest way to identify the bottleneck stage: the widest bar in the timeline is the slowest stage, and that is where you focus your investigation.
Reading Stage Metrics

Click a stage to see its detail page. The Summary Metrics table shows percentile distributions (min, 25th, median, 75th, max) for task duration, GC time, input size, output size, shuffle read, shuffle write, and spill. The key diagnostic ratios: max/median duration ratio > 5x indicates skew. GC time > 15% of task duration indicates memory pressure. Shuffle write significantly larger than input size indicates that the shuffle includes unnecessary columns. Spill (disk) > 0 indicates partition-level memory overflow. Each ratio points to a specific bottleneck category from lesson 1.
The Task Distribution Histogram

Below the summary metrics, the Stages tab shows a task list and optional histogram views. The task duration histogram is the most informative visualization for diagnosing skew: in a healthy stage, the histogram shows a tight cluster around the median duration. In a skewed stage, it shows a long tail — most tasks near the median and one or a few outliers at 5–50x the median. The outlier tasks correspond to the oversized partitions. NordGrid's on-call engineers sort the task list by duration descending to find the outlier tasks, then examine those tasks' input sizes to confirm whether the bottleneck is data skew (one task's input is much larger) or compute skew (all tasks have similar input but one takes much longer, indicating a UDF or data-dependent computation cost).
The Timeline View

The timeline view shows when each task started and finished across all executors. In a well-balanced stage, all tasks start at roughly the same time and finish at roughly the same time, forming a rectangular block. In a skewed stage, most tasks finish early and one task runs long, creating a single horizontal line that extends far beyond the rectangular block. In a stage with insufficient parallelism, tasks run in waves — the first batch finishes, then the second batch starts — because there are more tasks than available slots. The timeline makes cluster utilization visible: idle executor-seconds (the white space between the rectangular block and the long-running task) represent wasted compute that the pipeline paid for but did not use.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's stage analysis workflow: (1) open the Jobs tab, find the longest-running job, (2) click the job to see its stage DAG, identify the longest stage, (3) click the stage to see metrics, check max/median duration ratio, GC%, and spill, (4) if skewed: examine the outlier tasks' input sizes. If memory-bound: check GC% and spill. If shuffle-bound: check shuffle read/write volume. This 60-second workflow identifies the bottleneck category for any stage.

sections.title: Spark UI Deep Dive — SQL Tab

blocks.content: The SQL/DataFrame tab shows the execution plan as a visual DAG after execution, annotated with actual metrics for every operator. While the Stages tab shows you where the time was spent, the SQL tab shows you why: which operators processed how many rows, how much data was read from files, whether predicate pushdown worked, and which join strategy Catalyst chose. The SQL tab is the bridge between the text plan (Module 1) and the runtime reality: the text plan tells you what Spark intended to do, and the SQL tab tells you what Spark actually did, with actual row counts that may differ dramatically from Catalyst's estimates.
Reading the Operator DAG

Each query (triggered by an action) appears as a row in the SQL tab. Clicking it reveals the operator DAG: a tree of boxes representing physical operators, with arrows showing data flow from bottom (scan) to top (output). Each box shows the operator name and, when expanded, the actual metrics: number of output rows, data size, and time. The key information for performance diagnosis is the row count at each operator: a scan that reads 10 million rows followed by a filter that outputs 100,000 rows tells you the filter has 99% selectivity — excellent for pushdown. A join that takes 1 million rows from each side and produces 50 million rows tells you there are many-to-many matches — a sign of duplicate keys that may need investigation.
Comparing Estimated vs Actual Row Counts

Catalyst's cost-based optimizer estimates row counts at compile time to choose join strategies and operator ordering. The SQL tab shows both the estimate and the actual count for every operator. When these diverge significantly, Catalyst may have chosen the wrong strategy. NordGrid experienced this when the CBO estimated a filtered DataFrame at 5 million rows (based on stale statistics) but the actual count was 50,000 rows (99% selectivity). The estimate was 100x too high, causing Catalyst to choose a sort-merge join instead of a broadcast. The fix was running ANALYZE TABLE to update the statistics (Module 1, lesson 7). The SQL tab made the discrepancy visible: the estimated row count on the Exchange operator said 5M, but the actual said 50K.
Identifying Scan Volume

The FileScan operator in the SQL DAG shows three critical metrics: number of files read (how many Parquet files were opened), size of files read (total bytes from storage), and number of output rows (rows after pushdown filters). Comparing the size of files read against the total table size tells you how effective partition pruning is. If the table is 3.2TB and the scan reads 3.2TB, no pruning is happening. If it reads 8GB, pruning is working and reading only 0.25% of the data. NordGrid's performance dashboard tracks the 'scan ratio' (bytes read / total table bytes) for every pipeline run and alerts when it exceeds expected values.

# Programmatic access to SQL metrics (alternative to UI inspection)
# After running the query, access the last execution's metrics:

df = (spark.read.format('delta').load('silver/readings/')
    .filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
    .groupBy('meter_id').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw'))
)
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('gold/north_summary/')

# Access metrics from the Spark listener
# For automated monitoring, log these metrics to the pipeline metadata table
status_tracker = spark.sparkContext.statusTracker
for job_id in status_tracker.getJobIdsForGroup():
    job_info = status_tracker.getJobInfo(job_id)
    print(f"Job {job_id}: {job_info}")

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid checks the SQL tab after every pipeline change that affects reads or joins. The three metrics to verify: (1) FileScan size matches expected pruned size (not the full table), (2) Join operator matches expected strategy (BroadcastHashJoin for small tables), (3) Estimated vs actual row counts are within 10x of each other (if not, run ANALYZE TABLE). This 90-second check prevents the performance regressions that silently accumulate when nobody looks at the plan.

The Executors tab shows the cluster-level view: how many executors are active, how much memory and disk each is using, how many tasks each has completed, and the per-executor GC time, shuffle read/write, and input/output sizes. While the Stages tab diagnoses what is slow, the Executors tab diagnoses whether the cluster is being used efficiently. An executor with zero completed tasks means it was allocated but never received work — wasted resources. An executor with 90% GC time means it is spending most of its CPU on garbage collection instead of actual computation. An executor with 10x more shuffle read than the average means it is processing a skewed partition. The Executors tab turns the abstract concept of cluster utilization into concrete, per-executor numbers.
Key Executor Metrics

rows: How many tasks this executor ran
If one executor has 0 tasks, it is allocated but unused. Reduce executor count.
Total time spent in garbage collection
> 15%: executor memory too low or too much data cached. Increase memory or reduce cache.
One executor with 10x others = data skew. Apply AQE or salting.
One executor writing much more = input data skew before the shuffle.
> 80%: too much cached. Unpersist unused DataFrames.
Local disk used for shuffle spill and cache overflow
> 0: spill is occurring. Increase partitions or memory.

blocks.content: Detecting Uneven Utilization

The Executors tab is the fastest way to detect data skew at the cluster level without drilling into individual stages. If Executor 3 has completed 50 tasks and Executor 7 has completed 150 tasks, the task distribution is uneven. This can happen because of data skew (Executor 7 keeps getting the oversized partitions) or because of straggler behavior (Executor 3 is on a slow node with disk issues). The distinction matters: skew is fixed by changing the partition distribution (AQE, salting), while straggler behavior is fixed by enabling speculative execution (spark.speculation = true, which re-runs slow tasks on other executors). NordGrid enables speculative execution only for the historical reprocessing job where stragglers have been observed; the daily pipeline runs on a dedicated cluster where straggler behavior is rare.
GC Analysis

Garbage collection (GC) time above 15% of task time indicates that the JVM is spending more time managing memory than processing data. This happens when the executor's heap is nearly full — typically from oversized partitions, excessive caching, or large broadcast variables competing for the same heap space. The Executors tab shows total GC time per executor. If all executors have high GC, the cluster-wide executor memory is too low for the workload. If one executor has high GC and others are normal, that executor is processing an oversized partition (skew). NordGrid's monitoring dashboard tracks the maximum GC percentage across all executors for every run. If it exceeds 15%, the next capacity review evaluates whether to increase executor memory or reduce the per-task data volume through higher partition counts.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's executor health check after every pipeline run: (1) Tasks Completed column — are all executors contributing equally? (2) GC Time — is any executor above 15%? (3) Shuffle Read — is any executor reading 5x+ more than others? If all three checks pass, the cluster is healthy. If any fails, the team investigates before the next capacity review. This 30-second check catches utilization problems that do not manifest as duration regressions until data grows further.

The Spark UI is invaluable for debugging but impractical for continuous monitoring: it requires manual inspection, it disappears when the application terminates (unless history server is configured), and it cannot trigger alerts. Pipeline instrumentation solves this by embedding performance metrics directly into the pipeline code and writing them to a persistent monitoring table. After every run, the pipeline records its duration, row counts, shuffle volume, and quality check results. These metrics feed a dashboard that shows trends over time and an alerting system that fires when metrics deviate from baselines. This lesson covers NordGrid's instrumentation framework and the metrics table that drives their performance governance.
The Metrics Table

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, LongType, DoubleType, TimestampType

METRICS_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('pipeline_name',    StringType(),    False),
    StructField('stage_name',       StringType(),    False),
    StructField('process_date',     StringType(),    False),
    StructField('duration_seconds', DoubleType(),    True),
    StructField('input_row_count',  LongType(),      True),
    StructField('output_row_count', LongType(),      True),
    StructField('shuffle_bytes',    LongType(),      True),
    StructField('spill_bytes',      LongType(),      True),
    StructField('max_task_duration_ms', LongType(),  True),
    StructField('median_task_duration_ms', LongType(), True),
    StructField('recorded_at',      TimestampType(), False),
])
Instrumenting a Pipeline Stage

import time
from datetime import datetime
from pyspark.sql.functions import lit, current_timestamp

class StageMetrics:
    """Capture and record metrics for a pipeline stage."""
    
    def __init__(self, spark, pipeline_name, stage_name, process_date):
        self.spark = spark
        self.pipeline_name = pipeline_name
        self.stage_name = stage_name
        self.process_date = process_date
        self.start_time = None
        self.input_count = 0
        self.output_count = 0
    
    def start(self):
        self.start_time = time.time()
        return self
    
    def record_input(self, df):
        self.input_count = df.count()
        return self
    
    def record_output(self, df):
        self.output_count = df.count()
        return self
    
    def finish(self):
        duration = time.time() - self.start_time
        metrics_row = self.spark.createDataFrame([(
            self.pipeline_name, self.stage_name, self.process_date,
            duration, self.input_count, self.output_count,
            None, None, None, None,  # shuffle/spill/task metrics from UI
            datetime.now(),
        )], METRICS_SCHEMA)
        
        metrics_row.write.format('delta').mode('append').save('monitoring/pipeline_metrics/')
        print(f"  {self.stage_name}: {duration:.1f}s, {self.input_count:,} in, {self.output_count:,} out")

# Usage in the pipeline
metrics = StageMetrics(spark, 'silver_pipeline', 'bronze_to_silver', '2026-03-22')
metrics.start()
df_input = spark.read.format('delta').load(config['bronze_path'])
metrics.record_input(df_input)
df_output = apply_silver_transforms(df_input)
df_output.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save(config['silver_path'])
metrics.record_output(df_output)
metrics.finish()

The metrics table accumulates one row per stage per run. After 90 days of daily runs across 4 pipeline stages, the table has 360 rows — small enough to query instantly. NordGrid's monitoring dashboard reads from this table to plot duration trends, row count trends, and flag runs where the output/input ratio is anomalous (indicating that more or fewer rows were filtered than expected). The dashboard is a simple SQL query over the Delta table, refreshed every 15 minutes. No separate monitoring infrastructure is needed — the pipeline monitors itself using the same Delta Lake technology it processes data with.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid instruments every pipeline stage with the StageMetrics class. The overhead is minimal: two count() calls (one for input, one for output) and one small append to the metrics table. The total instrumentation cost is under 5 seconds per pipeline run. The monitoring value is enormous: the metrics table is the single source of truth for pipeline health, the basis for SLA tracking, and the first place the on-call engineer checks when an alert fires.

Module 3 covered join performance and shuffle optimization from a design perspective: which join strategy to choose, how to handle skew, and how to reduce shuffle volume. This lesson covers the operational perspective: when a production pipeline's shuffle stage is slow, how do you diagnose the root cause and determine the correct fix? The diagnostic workflow uses the Spark UI metrics from lessons 2–4 and applies them to the specific case of shuffle-dominated bottlenecks. NordGrid's monthly aggregation pipeline became shuffle-bound after the region expansion from 4 to 12 regions: the shuffle volume tripled but the partition count stayed at 200, producing uneven partition sizes that caused spill.
The Shuffle Diagnostic Workflow

rows: What is the total shuffle volume?
If volume > 2x the input data size, unnecessary columns are being shuffled. Add select() before the shuffle.
If too few: large partitions cause spill. If too many: empty partitions waste scheduling time.
Stages tab → Summary Metrics → max/median Shuffle Read
If max/median > 5x: data skew. Apply AQE or salting (Module 3).
If > 0: partitions exceed memory. Increase partition count or executor memory.
Can pre-filtering reduce the shuffle input?
SQL tab → FileScan rows vs Exchange input rows
If FileScan rows >> Exchange input rows, pre-filtering is working. If equal, no filter is applied before the shuffle.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Monthly Aggregation Fix

The monthly aggregation pipeline groups Silver readings by region, month, and meter_id to compute monthly consumption per meter. After the 4-to-12 region expansion, the shuffle volume increased from 3GB to 9GB (3x more data across 3x more regions). With 200 shuffle partitions, each partition averaged 45MB — acceptable. But the distribution was uneven: the NORTH region's partition held 300MB (the largest region with 3x the meters), causing spill on the executor processing it. The diagnostic workflow identified the problem at step 3 (max/median shuffle read ratio of 7x) and step 4 (300MB spill on the NORTH partition). The fix: enable AQE's skew handling (already enabled cluster-wide) and reduce spark.sql.shuffle.partitions from 200 to 48 (matching the 12 regions × 4 partitions per region target). The reduced partition count produced larger but more evenly distributed partitions because AQE split the NORTH partition while coalescing the empty ones.

# Before: 200 shuffle partitions, uneven distribution, 300MB spill on NORTH
# shuffle_partitions=200, max_task_duration=180s, median_task_duration=12s, ratio=15x

# After: 48 shuffle partitions with AQE, even distribution, zero spill
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '48')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
# Result: max_task_duration=35s, median_task_duration=28s, ratio=1.25x
# Stage duration: 180s → 72s (60% reduction)

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: The shuffle diagnostic workflow takes 2–3 minutes and identifies the root cause in 95% of shuffle-related slowdowns. NordGrid runs it whenever a pipeline's shuffle stage duration exceeds the 30-day baseline by more than 50%. The most common fix is adjusting the partition count (step 2). The second most common is adding pre-filtering (step 5). Skew (step 3) is the third most common and the most impactful to fix.

A scan-bound pipeline spends most of its time reading data from storage. The Spark UI shows a long FileScan stage with large input sizes and minimal time in subsequent stages. This pattern means the bottleneck is the I/O: the pipeline reads more data than it needs, and the transformations that follow are fast. The fix is always the same principle: read less data. But the specific fix depends on why Spark is reading too much: missing partition pruning (reading all partitions instead of the target subset), missing column pruning (reading all columns instead of the needed few), small files (metadata overhead dominates data read), or wrong file format (CSV instead of Parquet). This lesson provides the scan diagnostic workflow and NordGrid's incident where a dashboard query scanned 500GB instead of 15GB.
The Scan Diagnostic Workflow

rows: If empty or missing the expected filter: add explicit filter on partition columns. Check for UDFs that prevent pushdown.
If ReadSchema includes unused columns: add explicit select() before the operation that triggers the scan.
How many files are being read?
SQL tab → FileScan → number of files read
If > 10,000: small files problem. Run OPTIMIZE or compaction (Module 2, lesson 7; Module 4, lesson 9).
FileScan operator name (FileScan parquet vs FileScan csv)
If CSV or JSON: convert to Parquet for columnar pruning and compression.
If empty: the filter condition uses a UDF or non-pushable expression. Rewrite with built-in functions.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s 500GB Scan Incident

NordGrid's analytics team wrote a dashboard query that computed the average daily consumption for the NORTH region in March. The query filtered on region = 'NORTH' and date between '2026-03-01' and '2026-03-31'. The expected scan volume was 15GB (one region, one month). The actual scan volume was 500GB (all regions, all months). The scan diagnostic workflow identified the problem at step 1: PartitionFilters was empty. The investigation revealed that the dashboard framework constructed the query with the date filter as a Python string variable passed through expr(), not as a Column expression. The expr() call produced a string that Spark could not push down as a partition filter. Rewriting the filter as col('date').between('2026-03-01', '2026-03-31') restored partition pruning and reduced the scan from 500GB to 15GB — a 33x reduction.

The incident also revealed a column pruning failure: the dashboard query selected all columns (SELECT *) even though the visualization needed only region, date, and avg(daily_kw). The ReadSchema showed all 11 Silver columns instead of the 3 needed. Changing SELECT * to an explicit select('region', 'date', 'daily_kw') reduced the per-file read by 73% because Parquet's columnar format skips the byte ranges for the 8 unused columns entirely. Combined with the partition pruning fix, the total scan reduction was from 500GB to 4GB — a 125x improvement. The query dropped from 3 minutes to 1.4 seconds.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's scan efficiency metric: scan_ratio = bytes_read / total_table_bytes. For a query that filters to one region and one month from a 3.2TB three-year archive, the expected scan_ratio is approximately 0.004 (15GB / 3200GB). If the actual ratio exceeds the expected by 5x or more, the scan diagnostic workflow runs. The scan_ratio is recorded in the pipeline metrics table (lesson 5) and tracked on the monitoring dashboard. A rising scan_ratio over time indicates that pruning optimizations are being lost — usually due to new code that adds UDF-based filters or removes explicit select() calls.

Modules 1–8 introduced many Spark configuration properties in the context of specific topics: shuffle partitions in Module 2, broadcast threshold in Module 3, AQE settings in Module 1, executor memory in Module 8. This lesson consolidates them into a single reference: the ten most impactful configuration properties, their default values, what they control, when to change them, and NordGrid's recommended values for each pipeline category. The goal is a one-page reference that an engineer can consult when tuning any pipeline without re-reading four modules.
The Ten Most Impactful Properties

rows: Number of partitions after every shuffle (groupBy, join)
Max table size for automatic broadcast join
Enables AQE: partition coalescing, join conversion, skew handling
Partition must be Nx median to be considered skewed
Off-heap memory for Python, NIO, etc.
Auto-scale executor count based on workload
Scan all file footers to merge schemas

blocks.content: Tuning Order

When a pipeline needs tuning, NordGrid adjusts properties in a specific order based on impact and cost. First tier (free, high impact): spark.sql.shuffle.partitions and spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold. These are configuration changes that affect data distribution without any cluster cost. Second tier (moderate cost): spark.executor.memory and spark.executor.cores. These require cluster capacity but do not change the cluster size. Third tier (high cost): spark.executor.instances / dynamic allocation min/max. These affect the number of executors and thus the cluster cost. The rule: exhaust cheaper tiers before moving to more expensive ones. Most performance problems are solved at the first tier by adjusting partition count and broadcast threshold. Only persistent problems that survive first-tier tuning justify increasing executor resources.
Environment-Specific Overrides

NordGrid's YAML configuration (Module 5, lesson 7) stores environment-specific values for each property. Development uses low-resource values to conserve the shared dev cluster: 4 shuffle partitions, 2g executor memory, 1 executor core, dynamic allocation off. Staging mirrors production values exactly to ensure that staging validation (Module 7, lesson 4) tests the same execution behavior. Production uses the tuned values from the table above. This three-tier configuration prevents the common problem of tuning in development (small data, different resource profile) and finding that the tuning does not transfer to production (large data, different cluster).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's tuning checklist when a pipeline exceeds its SLA: (1) Check shuffle partitions — are they appropriate for the current data volume? (2) Check broadcast threshold — is a small table being sort-merged instead of broadcast? (3) Check AQE — is it enabled and handling skew? If these three checks do not resolve the issue, escalate to memory and resource tuning. Print this table and pin it next to the Spark UI bookmark.

A performance regression is a degradation that develops over time rather than appearing as a sudden failure. NordGrid's Silver pipeline gradually slowed from 7 minutes to 14 minutes over three months as the meter fleet grew from 1.0M to 1.2M meters. No single day's increase was noticeable — the pipeline took 10 seconds longer each day — but the cumulative effect doubled the runtime. Without automated regression detection, the team would not have noticed until the pipeline breached its 60-minute SLA, at which point the fix would be urgent instead of planned. Regression detection turns gradual degradation into early warnings that the team addresses proactively during capacity reviews.
What to Track

NordGrid tracks four time-series metrics from the pipeline metrics table (lesson 5): total duration (wall-clock seconds per run), total shuffle volume (bytes shuffled across all stages), maximum spill (bytes spilled in the worst stage), and scan ratio (bytes read / total table bytes). Each metric has a 30-day rolling average as its baseline. An alert fires when any metric exceeds the baseline by a configurable threshold: 2x for duration (the pipeline took twice as long as the average), 3x for shuffle volume (a sudden data growth or a missing filter), 5x for spill (a significant memory problem), and 2x for scan ratio (pruning degradation). The thresholds are deliberately conservative: they catch genuine regressions without firing on normal day-to-day variation.
Automated Alerts

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, avg, max as spark_max, when, lit

def check_regressions(spark, pipeline_name, current_run_date):
    """Compare today's metrics against the 30-day baseline. Alert on regression."""
    metrics = spark.read.format('delta').load('monitoring/pipeline_metrics/')
    
    # Current run metrics
    current = (metrics
        .filter(col('pipeline_name') == pipeline_name)
        .filter(col('process_date') == current_run_date)
    )
    
    # 30-day baseline (excluding today)
    baseline = (metrics
        .filter(col('pipeline_name') == pipeline_name)
        .filter(col('process_date') < current_run_date)
        .filter(col('process_date') >= date_sub(lit(current_run_date), 30))
        .agg(
            avg('duration_seconds').alias('avg_duration'),
            avg('shuffle_bytes').alias('avg_shuffle'),
            spark_max('spill_bytes').alias('max_spill_baseline'),
        )
    )
    
    current_vals = current.collect()[0]
    baseline_vals = baseline.collect()[0]
    
    alerts = []
    if current_vals['duration_seconds'] > baseline_vals['avg_duration'] * 2:
        alerts.append(f"Duration regression: {current_vals['duration_seconds']:.0f}s "
                      f"vs baseline {baseline_vals['avg_duration']:.0f}s")
    
    if alerts:
        send_slack_alert(pipeline_name, alerts)  # NordGrid's alerting channel
    else:
        print(f"No regressions detected for {pipeline_name} on {current_run_date}")
    
    return alerts
CI-Based Regression Detection

Production regression detection catches problems after deployment. CI-based regression detection catches them before deployment. NordGrid's CI pipeline (Module 7, lesson 7) runs the full pipeline on a 1,000-row synthetic dataset and records the execution plan and metrics. A CI regression test compares the current PR's plan against the baseline plan: did the join strategy change? Did the number of shuffle stages change? Did the scan volume on the fixture data change? If any plan characteristic changed unexpectedly, the CI test fails with a message explaining the difference. This prevents plan regressions (like accidentally breaking pushdown) from reaching production. The test takes 30 seconds and catches approximately 5% of PRs with unintended plan changes.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid runs regression detection at two levels: CI (pre-deployment, plan-based, catches code changes that degrade the execution plan) and production (post-deployment, metrics-based, catches data growth and environmental changes that degrade runtime). Both are automated. CI regression detection is a pytest test in the integration test suite. Production regression detection runs after every pipeline run and writes alerts to Slack. Together, they provide continuous performance assurance from development through production.

This lesson synthesizes the module into NordGrid's performance governance framework: the SLA definitions, the monitoring infrastructure, the escalation path when SLAs are breached, the quarterly tuning review process, and the performance incident retrospective template. The framework is the operational system that keeps 6 daily pipelines and 3 weekly pipelines running within their SLAs as data volume grows 20% per year. Without governance, individual pipelines drift until they breach SLAs. With governance, degradation is detected early and addressed during planned capacity reviews rather than during 3 AM incidents.
SLA Definitions
The Escalation Path

When a pipeline's duration exceeds its SLA, NordGrid follows a three-level escalation path. Level 1 (automated): the regression detection system sends a Slack alert to the #data-engineering channel. The on-call engineer has 30 minutes to acknowledge. Level 2 (on-call engineer): the engineer runs the diagnostic workflow (lesson 1–7 techniques) to identify the bottleneck, applies the fix, and re-runs the pipeline. If the pipeline cannot be fixed within the SLA's remaining headroom, the engineer escalates to Level 3. Level 3 (team response): the data engineering lead and the platform team join. The options include emergency cluster scaling, manual data recovery using Delta time travel, and temporary SLA relaxation with stakeholder notification. Level 3 has been triggered twice in 14 months: once during the region expansion (a planned capacity increase that was insufficiently sized) and once during a cloud storage outage (not a Spark issue).
The Quarterly Tuning Review
