Every quarter, the data engineering team conducts a tuning review using the metrics table from lesson 5. The review follows four steps: (1) plot the 90-day trend for each pipeline's duration, shuffle volume, spill, and scan ratio, (2) identify pipelines where any metric has increased by more than 30% over the quarter, (3) for each flagged pipeline, run the diagnostic workflow to determine whether the increase is from data growth (expected and manageable) or from a code or configuration issue (unexpected and actionable), (4) adjust partition counts, broadcast thresholds, or executor resources for the next quarter based on projected data growth. The review takes 2–3 hours and produces a configuration change plan that is deployed through the standard CI/CD process (Module 7). NordGrid's team considers the quarterly review the most valuable recurring meeting because it prevents the slow drift that causes unplanned incidents.
What Comes Next

Modules 8–9 complete the first two-thirds of the operations phase: memory management (Module 8) and performance monitoring (this module). Module 10 — the final module of the mid-level track — introduces Structured Streaming: Spark's unified batch-streaming model for near-real-time data processing. The streaming module builds on every concept in the track: the DataFrame API (junior track), execution plans (Module 1), partitioning (Module 2), Delta Lake (Module 4), idempotent processing (Module 5), testing (Module 6), and monitoring (this module) all apply directly to streaming pipelines with minor adaptations. Module 10 closes the track by unifying batch and streaming into a single architecture.

blocks.title: Module 9 complete
blocks.content: You can now approach performance tuning systematically with the measure-diagnose-fix-verify cycle. You can interpret the Spark UI's Jobs, Stages, SQL, and Executors tabs to identify bottleneck categories (shuffle, scan, memory). You can instrument pipelines with the StageMetrics class to capture duration, row counts, and quality metrics. You can diagnose slow shuffles and slow scans with targeted diagnostic workflows. You know the ten most impactful Spark configuration properties and the order to tune them. And you can detect performance regressions both in CI (plan-based) and in production (metrics-based). NordGrid's performance governance — SLA definitions, escalation path, quarterly tuning review — provides the operational framework for sustained performance at scale.

chapters.title: Structured Streaming Foundations
chapters.description: Understand Spark’s unified batch-streaming model, build micro-batch streaming pipelines for near-real-time data processing, and handle the production concerns of triggers, watermarks, and output modes.

blocks.content: For nine modules, every pipeline you have built processes data in batch: read a complete dataset, transform it, write the output. The pipeline runs once (or once per day), processes all available data, and terminates. NordGrid's daily batch pipeline starts at 6 AM, processes yesterday's 1.2 million meter readings in 7 minutes, and the operations dashboard refreshes at 7:30 AM with the updated data. This 24-hour latency was acceptable when NordGrid managed a stable grid. With the expansion to 1.2 million meters across 12 regions and the addition of 5,000 wind turbines whose output fluctuates with weather, the operations team now needs consumption data every 15 minutes to detect anomalies, balance load, and respond to demand spikes. Batch processing with a 24-hour cycle cannot meet this requirement. Streaming can.
What Streaming Means in Practice

In a streaming pipeline, data arrives continuously and the pipeline processes it incrementally as it arrives rather than waiting for the entire dataset to be available. NordGrid's meter collection system drops new Parquet files into a Bronze directory every 15 minutes, each containing approximately 75,000 readings (1/16 of the daily 1.2M). A streaming pipeline monitors that directory, detects new files as they appear, reads them, transforms them using the same Silver logic from the batch pipeline, and writes the results to a Silver Delta table. The operations dashboard reads from the Silver table and refreshes. The end-to-end latency from meter reading to dashboard update drops from 24 hours to approximately 2 minutes: 15 minutes for the file to arrive, 30 seconds for the streaming pipeline to process it, and 60 seconds for the dashboard to refresh.
Structured Streaming — The Unified Model

Spark's Structured Streaming is not a separate framework from the DataFrame API you already know. It extends the same API with streaming capabilities. You use spark.readStream instead of spark.read. You use df.writeStream instead of df.write. The transformations in between — filter, select, withColumn, groupBy, join — are identical to the batch versions. This unified model means that the transformation logic, the quality gates, the schema enforcement, and the testing patterns from Modules 1–9 apply directly to streaming pipelines with only minor adaptations. You do not need to learn a new framework. You need to learn the streaming-specific concepts (triggers, output modes, watermarks, checkpoints) that control how the familiar DataFrame operations execute in a continuous context.

left.label: Batch Processing
points: Runs once, processes all data, terminates
Data is bounded (known size at start)

right.label: Structured Streaming
points: Runs continuously, processes new data as it arrives

blocks.content: The similarity is not superficial. Under the hood, Structured Streaming executes each micro-batch as a small batch query: it reads the new data since the last trigger, applies the DataFrame transformations, and writes the output. Catalyst optimizes each micro-batch exactly as it optimizes a batch query — predicate pushdown, column pruning, join strategy selection, and AQE all apply. The execution plans you learned to read in Module 1 are the same plans you will see in streaming queries. The partition tuning from Module 2, the join optimization from Module 3, and the memory management from Module 8 all apply to the micro-batch execution. Streaming is batch at a higher frequency, not a fundamentally different paradigm.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's streaming strategy: do not replace the batch pipeline with a streaming pipeline. Run both. The daily batch pipeline produces the authoritative Silver table (complete, validated, quality-gated). The streaming pipeline produces a near-real-time Silver table for the operations dashboard (low-latency, best-effort). The batch pipeline corrects any gaps or errors the streaming pipeline missed. This dual-pipeline architecture provides both low latency and high correctness — the streaming pipeline optimizes for speed, and the batch pipeline optimizes for accuracy.

A streaming DataFrame is created with spark.readStream instead of spark.read. The readStream method returns a DataFrame that looks identical to a batch DataFrame — it has a schema, it supports the same transformations, and it prints the same column names and types. The critical difference is that a streaming DataFrame represents an unbounded table: new rows are appended as data arrives. You can apply filter(), select(), withColumn(), and most other transformations to it exactly as you would to a batch DataFrame. But you cannot call actions like count() or collect() because the table has no defined end — the count would change with every new micro-batch. Instead, you use writeStream to define a continuous output that processes each micro-batch and writes the results.
Creating a Streaming DataFrame

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType, TimestampType

# Explicit schema is REQUIRED for streaming reads (no inferSchema)
meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id',  StringType(), False),
    StructField('daily_kw',  DoubleType(), True),
    StructField('date',      DateType(),   False),
    StructField('region',    StringType(), False),
    StructField('timestamp', TimestampType(), False),
])

# Streaming read from a directory of Parquet files
df_stream = (spark.readStream
    .schema(meter_schema)            # schema is mandatory for streaming
    .parquet('bronze/readings/')     # monitors this directory for new files
)

print(f"Is streaming: {df_stream.isStreaming}")  # True
print(f"Schema: {df_stream.schema}")
# The DataFrame exists but no data has been read yet —
# data flows when writeStream.start() is called
Schema Requirement

Unlike batch reads where inferSchema can scan the data to determine types, streaming reads require an explicit schema. The reason is fundamental: a streaming source has no complete dataset to scan at read time — the first micro-batch may contain only a fraction of the eventual schema variations. If Spark inferred the schema from the first batch and a later batch had a different schema, the pipeline would fail or produce wrong results. The explicit schema ensures consistency across all micro-batches regardless of when they arrive or what data they contain. NordGrid defines the streaming schema as a constant in the schemas module (the same constant used by the batch pipeline and the CI schema contract tests from Module 6), ensuring that batch and streaming pipelines expect identical input structures.
Streaming vs Static DataFrames

rows: Not supported (use console output mode for debugging)

blocks.content: The restrictions on streaming DataFrames exist because certain operations require seeing the complete dataset. sort() needs all rows to determine order. distinct() needs all rows to determine uniqueness. count() needs all rows to return a number. These operations are undefined on unbounded data because 'all rows' never exists — new rows keep arriving. The workaround for each restriction is to use a windowed aggregation (Module 10, lesson 7): instead of sorting all rows, you compute a windowed result. Instead of counting all rows, you count rows within a time window. The streaming API guides you toward windowed thinking, which is the natural way to reason about continuous data.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's streaming convention: define the streaming schema as a Python constant imported from the schemas module. The same constant is used by spark.readStream.schema(), by the batch pipeline's explicit schema, and by the CI schema contract test. This single-source-of-truth approach prevents the scenario where the streaming pipeline accepts data that the batch pipeline rejects (or vice versa).

When a streaming query writes output, it must decide what to write for each micro-batch. The output mode determines this. Append mode writes only the new rows from the current micro-batch — rows that did not exist in the previous output. Complete mode rewrites the entire result table after each micro-batch — every row, not just new ones. Update mode writes only the rows that changed since the last micro-batch. Each mode has constraints on which operations it supports, and choosing the wrong mode produces either an error (Spark rejects the combination) or unexpected output (the sink receives data in a format the consumer does not expect). This lesson covers all three modes, their constraints, and when NordGrid uses each.
Append Mode

Append mode writes only new rows. It is the default mode and the most common for streaming pipelines that produce fact-like data: each micro-batch adds new meter readings, new events, or new log entries. The constraint: append mode cannot be used with aggregations that update previously emitted rows. A groupBy().count() where the count changes with each batch cannot use append mode because the count for a group emitted in batch 1 would need to be updated in batch 2 when new rows for that group arrive. Append mode works for stateless transformations (filter, select, withColumn) and for aggregations with watermarks that finalize results (lesson 7). NordGrid's streaming Silver pipeline uses append mode because each micro-batch adds new readings without modifying previous ones.
Complete Mode

Complete mode rewrites the entire result after each micro-batch. It is required for aggregations without watermarks: a groupBy('region').count() in complete mode outputs all 12 region counts after every batch, with the counts updated to include the new rows. The downside is that the entire result is rewritten every time, which is expensive for large result sets. Complete mode is appropriate when the result is small (12 rows for regional counts) and the consumer expects the full result (a dashboard that displays all regions). NordGrid's operations dashboard uses a streaming aggregation in complete mode: the 12-region consumption summary is recomputed every 15 minutes and written to a small Delta table that the dashboard reads.
Update Mode

Update mode writes only the rows that changed since the last micro-batch. For a groupBy('region').count(), update mode emits only the regions whose count changed in the current batch. If only NORTH and EAST received new data, update mode writes 2 rows (the updated NORTH and EAST counts), while complete mode writes all 12. Update mode is more efficient than complete mode for large result sets where each batch affects only a fraction of the groups. The constraint: update mode requires the output sink to support upserts (update-or-insert), because the sink must replace existing rows with updated values. Delta Lake supports this natively. Plain Parquet does not. NordGrid uses update mode for the billing detail streaming table (6 million groups where each batch updates a small subset).

rows: Streaming Silver readings (new rows every 15 min)
Requires small result set to be practical
Sinks that do not support upsert (plain Parquet)
Billing detail streaming table (updated groups only)

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's output mode selection: if the streaming pipeline produces row-level data without aggregation (Silver readings), use append. If it produces a small aggregated summary (regional dashboard), use complete. If it produces a large aggregated result where each batch affects few groups (billing details), use update with a Delta sink. When in doubt, start with append mode for non-aggregated output and complete mode for aggregated output. Switch to update mode only when complete mode's full-rewrite cost is unacceptable.

A trigger controls how often a streaming query checks for new data and processes a micro-batch. Without a trigger, the query processes micro-batches as fast as possible: as soon as one batch finishes, the next begins. This continuous processing maximizes throughput but also maximizes resource usage — the Spark application runs constantly even when no new data is arriving. In production, you almost always want a trigger that matches the data arrival rate. If new files arrive every 15 minutes, a 15-minute trigger processes each batch once and then waits, consuming resources only during the ~30-second processing window. Spark provides four trigger types that cover the range from continuous processing to one-shot batch-like execution.
The Four Trigger Types

rows: Process next batch immediately after current finishes
Never used in production (wastes resources during idle periods)
Process a batch every 15 minutes. If a batch takes longer than the interval, the next starts immediately.
Moderate — active during processing, idle between batches
Operations dashboard: matches the 15-minute file arrival rate
Process all available data in one batch, then stop the query
End-of-day reconciliation: process any remaining files, then stop
Process all available data in multiple batches, then stop
Moderate — processes available backlog, then terminates
Catch-up after a streaming outage: process the accumulated backlog, then stop

blocks.content: Configuring Triggers

from pyspark.sql.streaming import Trigger

df_stream = spark.readStream.schema(meter_schema).parquet('bronze/readings/')
df_transformed = apply_silver_transforms(df_stream)

# 15-minute trigger: process a batch every 15 minutes
query = (df_transformed.writeStream
    .outputMode('append')
    .format('delta')
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/silver_streaming/')
    .trigger(processingTime='15 minutes')
    .start('silver/readings_streaming/')
)

# Once trigger: process all available data, then stop
query_once = (df_transformed.writeStream
    .outputMode('append')
    .format('delta')
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/silver_once/')
    .trigger(once=True)
    .start('silver/readings_streaming/')
)

# availableNow: process backlog in multiple batches, then stop
query_catchup = (df_transformed.writeStream
    .outputMode('append')
    .format('delta')
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/silver_catchup/')
    .trigger(availableNow=True)
    .start('silver/readings_streaming/')
)
Checkpoint Location

Every streaming query requires a checkpoint location — a directory where Spark stores the query's progress: which files have been processed, which offsets have been consumed, and the intermediate state for stateful operations. The checkpoint enables exactly-once semantics: if the query fails and restarts, it reads the checkpoint to determine where it left off and resumes from that point without reprocessing already-handled data or skipping unprocessed data. The checkpoint location must be durable storage (HDFS, S3, ADLS) and must not be shared between different streaming queries. NordGrid stores checkpoints in a dedicated checkpoints/ directory on S3, with one subdirectory per streaming query. Deleting a checkpoint resets the query to the beginning — it will reprocess all available data, which is useful for backfills but dangerous if done accidentally.

blocks.title: Checkpoint is the streaming query’s state
blocks.content: Never delete a checkpoint directory unless you intentionally want to reprocess all data from scratch. Deleting the checkpoint causes the query to forget its progress and reprocess everything, which can duplicate data in the output sink. NordGrid protects checkpoint directories with restricted write permissions: only the streaming application's service account can modify them. Accidental checkpoint deletion caused a data duplication incident early in NordGrid's streaming adoption; the protection policy was implemented the same week.

The file-based streaming source is the most common entry point for teams transitioning from batch to streaming. Instead of reading a directory once (spark.read), you monitor it continuously (spark.readStream). When new files appear in the directory, Spark detects them and includes them in the next micro-batch. This works with any file format Spark supports: Parquet, CSV, JSON, ORC. For NordGrid, the meter collection system drops new Parquet files into the Bronze directory every 15 minutes, and the streaming Silver pipeline picks them up automatically. No code change is needed in the collection system — it continues writing files exactly as before. The change is on the processing side: from a daily batch read to a continuous streaming read.
File Streaming Configuration

# Streaming read from a directory of Parquet files
df_stream = (spark.readStream
    .schema(meter_schema)                      # explicit schema required
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', '10')         # process at most 10 files per batch
    .option('latestFirst', 'false')             # process oldest files first (FIFO)
    .option('cleanSource', 'off')               # do not delete processed files
    .parquet('bronze/readings/')                # monitor this directory
)

# For CSV files (NordGrid's partner data):
df_csv_stream = (spark.readStream
    .schema(partner_schema)
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', '5')
    .option('header', 'true')
    .csv('bronze/partner_readings/')
)
maxFilesPerTrigger

The maxFilesPerTrigger option limits how many new files are processed in each micro-batch. Without this limit, a streaming query that starts with a backlog of 1,000 files would try to process all 1,000 in the first batch — a massive batch that could overwhelm executor memory or exceed the trigger interval. Setting maxFilesPerTrigger = 10 ensures each batch processes at most 10 files, keeping the batch size predictable and the resource usage bounded. NordGrid sets maxFilesPerTrigger to 10 for the streaming Silver pipeline: at 15-minute intervals, this processes 10 files per batch. Since the collection system produces 4 files per 15 minutes (one per partition), the pipeline comfortably handles normal inflow and gradually works through any backlog from a restart.
File Discovery and New File Detection

Spark detects new files by comparing the directory listing against the set of files already processed (stored in the checkpoint). A file is 'new' if its path has not been seen before. This means that modifying an existing file does not trigger reprocessing — Spark does not re-read files it has already processed. If the collection system overwrites a file with corrected data, the streaming pipeline will not pick up the correction. NordGrid's convention: the collection system writes each batch to a new file with a unique name (including a timestamp: readings_20260322_061500.parquet). Corrections are written as new files, not as overwrites. This ensures the streaming pipeline processes every version of the data.
Writing the Streaming Output

# Complete streaming Silver pipeline: read, transform, write
df_stream = (spark.readStream
    .schema(meter_schema)
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', '10')
    .parquet('bronze/readings/')
)

# Apply the SAME transformation logic as the batch pipeline
df_silver = apply_silver_transforms(df_stream)  # reuse from batch

# Write to a streaming Delta table
query = (df_silver.writeStream
    .outputMode('append')
    .format('delta')
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/silver_streaming/')
    .trigger(processingTime='15 minutes')
    .start('silver/readings_streaming/')
)

# The query runs until stopped
query.awaitTermination()  # blocks until the query is stopped or errors

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's file streaming convention: always set maxFilesPerTrigger to a value that keeps each micro-batch under 60 seconds of processing time. If each file is 50MB and the pipeline processes 50MB in 5 seconds, maxFilesPerTrigger = 10 means 50 seconds per batch — well within the 15-minute trigger interval. This leaves 14 minutes of idle time per interval, which is headroom for backlog processing if the pipeline is temporarily unavailable.

The most powerful aspect of Structured Streaming is that the transformation API is identical to batch. The filter(), select(), withColumn(), and join() operations you have written throughout this track work on streaming DataFrames without modification. NordGrid's streaming Silver pipeline applies the same apply_silver_transforms() function that the batch pipeline uses — a single Python function that accepts a DataFrame (batch or streaming) and returns a transformed DataFrame. This code reuse is not a convenience; it is a correctness guarantee: both pipelines produce identical output for the same input because they use the same transformation logic. This lesson covers which transformations work in streaming, which have restrictions, and how NordGrid structures code for batch-streaming reuse.
Supported Transformations

All stateless transformations work identically on streaming DataFrames: filter() removes rows, select() chooses columns, withColumn() adds or replaces columns, drop() removes columns, and alias() renames. Type casts, string functions, date functions, numeric functions, conditional logic (when/otherwise), and all built-in functions from the junior-level track's Module 9 work without modification. UDFs also work, with the same performance caveat as in batch (serialization overhead). NordGrid's streaming Silver pipeline applies type casting (cast daily_kw to double), string cleaning (upper, trim on region), date extraction (year, month from date), conditional tier classification (when/otherwise), and metadata tagging (withColumn for _processed_at) — all using the same function the batch pipeline calls.
Joining Streaming with Static Data

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# Static reference tables (read once, used for every micro-batch)
df_meters = spark.read.format('delta').load('reference/meters/')         # 1.2M rows
df_substations = spark.read.format('delta').load('reference/substations/') # 200 rows

# Streaming readings
df_stream = (spark.readStream
    .schema(meter_schema)
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', '10')
    .parquet('bronze/readings/')
)

# Join streaming data with static reference tables
df_enriched = (df_stream
    .join(broadcast(df_substations), 'substation_id', 'left')  # broadcast the tiny table
    .join(df_meters, 'meter_id', 'left')                       # standard join with meters
)

# The join works identically to batch:
# - substations is broadcast (small table)
# - meters is shuffled (large table)
# - The static tables are read once and reused for every micro-batch
Restrictions

Three operations that work in batch do not work on streaming DataFrames. sort() and orderBy() are not supported because sorting requires seeing all rows, which is impossible on unbounded data. distinct() without aggregation is not supported for the same reason. dropDuplicates() with a watermark is the streaming alternative to distinct() — it deduplicates within a time window rather than across the entire dataset. These restrictions are Spark's way of preventing operations that would require unbounded state: if Spark had to track every row ever seen to compute distinct(), the state would grow indefinitely until the executor ran out of memory. Windowed operations (lesson 7) provide the bounded alternative.
The Shared Transformation Module

# pipeline/transforms.py — shared between batch and streaming entry points

def apply_silver_transforms(df):
    """
    Apply Silver-layer transformations.
    
    This function accepts BOTH static and streaming DataFrames.
    It uses only stateless operations (filter, select, withColumn)
    that are compatible with both execution modes.
    
    Do NOT add sort(), distinct(), or collect() here —
    these break streaming compatibility.
    """
    from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, trim, year, month, when, lit
    
    return (df
        .withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast('double'))
        .withColumn('region', upper(trim(col('region'))))
        .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
        .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
        .withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
        .withColumn('month', month(col('date')))
        .withColumn('tier',
            when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'low')
            .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'medium')
            .when(col('daily_kw') < 4000, 'high')
            .otherwise('critical')
        )
    )

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's code reuse rule: all transformation logic lives in pipeline/transforms.py. Both the batch entry point (pipeline_silver.py) and the streaming entry point (pipeline_silver_streaming.py) import and call the same functions. The transforms module never uses sort(), distinct(), or collect(), ensuring streaming compatibility. If a transform needs deduplication, it uses dropDuplicates() with a watermark in the streaming entry point and dropDuplicates() without a watermark in the batch entry point. The transform module provides the common logic; the entry points provide the mode-specific wrappers.

Batch aggregations are straightforward: groupBy('region').sum('daily_kw') processes all rows and produces a single result. Streaming aggregations are more complex because the data is unbounded: new rows keep arriving, and the aggregation result changes with each batch. The operations dashboard needs to show the total consumption per region for the current 15-minute window, not for all time. This requires windowed aggregation: group the data by time windows, compute the aggregate for each window, and emit the result when the window closes. The watermark concept determines when a window is 'closed' — when Spark can be confident that no more data will arrive for that window and the result is final.
Tumbling Window Aggregation

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, window, sum as spark_sum, count

df_stream = (spark.readStream
    .schema(meter_schema)
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', '10')
    .parquet('bronze/readings/')
)

# Tumbling window: 15-minute windows based on the event timestamp
df_windowed = (df_stream
    .withWatermark('timestamp', '30 minutes')   # allow up to 30 min late data
    .groupBy(
        window(col('timestamp'), '15 minutes'),  # 15-minute tumbling window
        col('region'),
    )
    .agg(
        spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
        count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
    )
)

# Write with append mode — finalized windows are appended to the output
query = (df_windowed.writeStream
    .outputMode('append')           # append: only finalized (closed) windows
    .format('delta')
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/regional_dashboard/')
    .trigger(processingTime='15 minutes')
    .start('gold/regional_dashboard_streaming/')
)
How Watermarks Work

A watermark tells Spark how long to wait for late-arriving data before finalizing a window. withWatermark('timestamp', '30 minutes') means: consider a window closed when the maximum event timestamp seen so far exceeds the window's end time by 30 minutes. If the current maximum timestamp is 14:45 and the window is [14:00, 14:15), the window is closed because 14:45 - 14:15 = 30 minutes, matching the watermark threshold. Any data arriving with a timestamp before 14:15 after this point is considered 'too late' and is dropped. The watermark is a trade-off between latency and completeness: a shorter watermark emits results faster but drops more late data, while a longer watermark waits longer but captures more late arrivals. NordGrid's 30-minute watermark accommodates the cellular communication delays that cause meter readings to arrive 10–20 minutes late.
Output Mode with Watermarked Aggregations

Watermarked aggregations support both append and update output modes, but with different semantics. In append mode, Spark emits the result only when the window is finalized (closed by the watermark). The output is delayed by the watermark duration but guaranteed to be complete. In update mode, Spark emits partial results as each micro-batch updates the running aggregate, and the final result is emitted when the window closes. Update mode provides lower latency (partial results are visible immediately) but requires the sink to handle updates. NordGrid uses append mode for the operations dashboard (the dashboard refreshes every 15 minutes anyway, so the watermark delay is invisible) and update mode for real-time alerts (where seeing partial consumption data immediately is more valuable than waiting for the complete window).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's watermark selection: set the watermark to the 99th percentile of data arrival latency plus a safety margin. If 99% of readings arrive within 20 minutes of their event time, set the watermark to 30 minutes (20 + 10 margin). This captures 99%+ of data in the windowed aggregation and drops only the extreme outliers. Monitor the late-data drop rate in the streaming metrics (lesson 9): if it exceeds 1%, increase the watermark. If it is near 0%, the watermark may be unnecessarily long, adding latency without benefit.

Joining streaming data with reference tables is the most common streaming join pattern. Lesson 6 showed the syntax: a streaming DataFrame joined with a static DataFrame using the same join() API as batch. This lesson covers the operational details: how Spark handles the static side (it reads it once and caches it for all micro-batches), how to refresh the static side when the reference data changes, and the conceptual introduction to stream-stream joins where both sides are streaming. NordGrid's streaming Silver pipeline joins streaming meter readings with three static reference tables (meters, substations, weather), and the reference tables are refreshed once daily by the batch pipeline.
Stream-Static Join Mechanics

When you join a streaming DataFrame with a static DataFrame, Spark reads the static side once when the query starts and caches it in executor memory. Every micro-batch's new rows are joined against this cached copy. The static data does not change between micro-batches — even if the underlying file is updated, the streaming query continues using the version it read at startup. This means that if the meters reference table is updated daily at 6 AM, the streaming query started at midnight still uses yesterday's version until the query is restarted. NordGrid handles this by restarting the streaming query after the daily batch completes. The restart is graceful: the query is stopped, the checkpoint records the progress, and the restarted query picks up new reference data and resumes from where it left off.

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# Stream-static join: streaming readings with static reference data
df_stream = spark.readStream.schema(meter_schema).parquet('bronze/readings/')
df_meters = spark.read.format('delta').load('reference/meters/')         # static
df_substations = spark.read.format('delta').load('reference/substations/') # static

# The static DataFrames are read once when the query starts
df_enriched = (df_stream
    .join(broadcast(df_substations), 'substation_id', 'left')
    .join(df_meters.select('meter_id', 'customer_type'), 'meter_id', 'left')
)

# Apply transforms and write
df_silver = apply_silver_transforms(df_enriched)
query = (df_silver.writeStream
    .outputMode('append')
    .format('delta')
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/silver_enriched/')
    .trigger(processingTime='15 minutes')
    .start('silver/readings_streaming/')
)
Refreshing Static Reference Data

NordGrid's Airflow DAG orchestrates the daily reference data refresh: (1) the batch pipeline updates the meters and substations Delta tables, (2) the DAG stops the streaming query gracefully (query.stop()), (3) the DAG restarts the streaming application. The restarted application reads the updated reference tables and resumes processing from the checkpoint. The restart takes approximately 30 seconds (SparkSession creation + static table read + checkpoint recovery), during which no micro-batches are processed. The data arrival continues during the restart, and the first micro-batch after restart picks up any files that arrived during the gap. No data is lost because the checkpoint tracks which files have been processed.
Stream-Stream Joins (Conceptual)

Stream-stream joins — where both sides are streaming DataFrames — are supported in Structured Streaming but are significantly more complex than stream-static joins. Both sides are unbounded, so Spark must buffer state for both and match rows as they arrive. Watermarks are required on both sides to bound the state and prevent unbounded memory growth. NordGrid does not currently use stream-stream joins; the team's architecture keeps reference data as static tables refreshed daily. Stream-stream joins become necessary when the reference data changes at streaming speed (for example, real-time pricing updates that must be joined with real-time meter readings). This is a senior-level topic that the senior track covers in depth.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid's reference data refresh strategy: static tables are refreshed daily via query restart. The restart is fast (~30 seconds), the checkpoint preserves progress, and no data is lost. This approach is simpler and more reliable than stream-stream joins for reference data that changes infrequently (daily or less). Only consider stream-stream joins when the reference data changes at sub-minute frequency and the latency of a daily restart is unacceptable.

A batch pipeline runs, completes, and reports success or failure. A streaming pipeline runs indefinitely, and its health must be monitored continuously. A streaming query can be running but unhealthy: processing slower than data arrives (backpressure), producing incorrect results (a stale static table), or silently dropping late data beyond the watermark. The StreamingQuery object provides the monitoring interface: status (is the query running?), recentProgress (metrics from the last few micro-batches), and lastProgress (detailed metrics from the most recent batch). NordGrid writes these metrics to the same pipeline metrics Delta table used by the batch pipeline (Module 9, lesson 5), enabling a unified monitoring dashboard for both batch and streaming pipelines.
The StreamingQuery API

# After starting a streaming query
query = df_silver.writeStream.format('delta') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', 'checkpoints/silver_streaming/') \
    .trigger(processingTime='15 minutes') \
    .start('silver/readings_streaming/')

# Check query status
print(query.status)
# {'message': 'Waiting for next trigger', 'isDataAvailable': False, 'isTriggerActive': False}

# Get metrics from the last micro-batch
progress = query.lastProgress
if progress:
    print(f"Batch ID: {progress['batchId']}")
    print(f"Input rows: {progress['numInputRows']}")
    print(f"Processing time: {progress['batchDuration']}ms")
    print(f"Input rate: {progress['inputRowsPerSecond']:.0f} rows/sec")
    print(f"Processing rate: {progress['processedRowsPerSecond']:.0f} rows/sec")
    print(f"Sources: {progress['sources']}")
Detecting Backpressure

Backpressure occurs when data arrives faster than the streaming query can process it. The symptom: the processing rate (rows processed per second) is lower than the input rate (rows arriving per second). The backlog of unprocessed data grows with each trigger interval. If the backlog grows indefinitely, the query falls further behind real-time until the latency becomes unacceptable. NordGrid detects backpressure by comparing inputRowsPerSecond with processedRowsPerSecond from the lastProgress metrics. If the ratio exceeds 1.5x for three consecutive batches, an alert fires. The fix depends on the bottleneck: increase maxFilesPerTrigger (if the query is not using its full trigger window), increase executor count (if the cluster is at capacity), or optimize the transformation logic (if the per-row processing cost is the bottleneck).
Writing Streaming Metrics

import time
import threading
from datetime import datetime

def monitor_streaming_query(query, spark, pipeline_name, interval_seconds=60):
    """Periodically write streaming metrics to the monitoring table."""
    while query.isActive:
        progress = query.lastProgress
        if progress:
            metrics = spark.createDataFrame([(
                pipeline_name,
                'streaming_batch',
                str(datetime.now().date()),
                progress.get('batchDuration', 0) / 1000.0,  # ms to seconds
                progress.get('numInputRows', 0),
                progress.get('numInputRows', 0),  # output = input for stateless
                None, None, None, None,
                datetime.now(),
            )], METRICS_SCHEMA)
            metrics.write.format('delta').mode('append').save('monitoring/pipeline_metrics/')
        time.sleep(interval_seconds)

# Start the monitor in a background thread
monitor_thread = threading.Thread(
    target=monitor_streaming_query,
    args=(query, spark, 'silver_streaming'),
    daemon=True,
)
monitor_thread.start()

The monitoring thread writes one row to the metrics table every 60 seconds. Each row captures the batch ID, input row count, processing duration, and timestamp. The same dashboard that monitors batch pipelines (Module 9) displays the streaming metrics alongside them. NordGrid's dashboard shows a time series of streaming batch durations: a flat line at 25–35 seconds indicates healthy processing, while spikes above the 15-minute trigger interval indicate batches that could not complete before the next trigger fired. The unified dashboard provides a single view of all pipeline health — batch and streaming — without requiring separate monitoring infrastructure.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: NordGrid monitors three streaming health signals: (1) query.isActive — is the query still running? An alert fires if the query stops unexpectedly. (2) inputRowsPerSecond vs processedRowsPerSecond — is backpressure developing? An alert fires if the ratio exceeds 1.5x for 3+ batches. (3) Watermark lag — how far behind real-time is the watermark? If it exceeds 1 hour, late data handling may be dropping too many rows. These three signals catch the most common streaming failure modes: query death, processing bottleneck, and data freshness degradation.

This is the final lesson of the mid-level PySpark track. It synthesizes Module 10 with the entire track by describing NordGrid's unified batch-streaming architecture: how the daily batch pipeline and the 15-minute streaming pipeline coexist, share transformation logic, write to the same Delta Lake tables, and are monitored by the same dashboard. The architecture demonstrates that streaming is not a replacement for batch but an extension: the batch pipeline provides the authoritative, quality-gated, complete dataset, while the streaming pipeline provides the near-real-time, best-effort, low-latency view. Together, they serve every consumer: the operations team (15-minute data), the billing team (daily data), and the regulatory team (monthly data).
The Unified Architecture

blocks.title: NordGrid batch-streaming unified architecture
blocks.content:
Source: Meter Collection System
│
├─► Files every 15 min ──► Bronze Directory (Parquet files)
│                              │
│                   ┌──────────┴──────────┐
│                   │                       │
│           Batch Pipeline            Streaming Pipeline
│           (daily, 6 AM)             (continuous, 15-min trigger)
│           spark.read                spark.readStream
│                   │                       │
│           apply_silver_transforms() ←─ SHARED MODULE ─► apply_silver_transforms()
│                   │                       │
│           Quality Gates              No quality gates (best-effort)
│                   │                       │
│           Silver Delta (auth)        Silver Delta (streaming)
│           ┌───────┴───────┐              │
│           │               │              │
│        Gold Summary    Gold Billing    Operations Dashboard
│        (batch)         (batch)         (streaming, 15-min refresh)
│
└─► Monitoring Dashboard (unified: batch + streaming metrics)
blocks.caption: Both pipelines read from the same Bronze directory and share the same transformation module. The batch pipeline produces the authoritative Silver table with quality gates. The streaming pipeline produces a near-real-time Silver table for the operations dashboard.

blocks.content: Shared Transformation Logic

The centerpiece of the architecture is the shared transforms module (pipeline/transforms.py). Both entry points — pipeline_silver.py (batch) and pipeline_silver_streaming.py (streaming) — import and call the same apply_silver_transforms() function. The function uses only stateless transformations (filter, select, withColumn, cast, when/otherwise) that work identically on both static and streaming DataFrames. Mode-specific logic is isolated in the entry points: the batch entry point adds quality gates, metadata tagging, and checkpoint management. The streaming entry point adds trigger configuration, checkpoint location, and output mode. The shared module contains zero streaming-specific code and zero batch-specific code — it is pure transformation logic that both modes execute.
The Reconciliation Pattern

The batch and streaming pipelines write to separate Delta tables: silver/readings/ (batch, authoritative) and silver/readings_streaming/ (streaming, near-real-time). The operations dashboard reads from the streaming table for 15-minute freshness. The billing and regulatory pipelines read from the batch table for accuracy and completeness. Every morning at 7:30 AM, after the batch pipeline completes, a reconciliation job compares the streaming table against the batch table for the previous day. Rows in the streaming table that do not match the batch table (due to late-arriving data, quality gate differences, or processing order effects) are flagged. Historically, the mismatch rate is under 0.1% — the streaming pipeline's best-effort output is nearly identical to the batch pipeline's authoritative output. The 0.1% discrepancy is acceptable for the operations dashboard but not for billing, which is why the dual-table architecture exists.
The Complete Mid-Level Toolkit

rows: Streaming micro-batches produce the same plans as batch queries
Stream-static joins use broadcast for small reference tables
Streaming writes to Delta with schema enforcement and checkpoints
Checkpoints provide exactly-once semantics. Quality gates in batch, not streaming.
Unit tests, integration tests, schema contracts
Shared transforms are tested once, valid for both batch and streaming
Streaming deployment includes query restart and checkpoint management
Streaming state (watermarks, aggregations) consumes executor memory
Streaming metrics feed the same dashboard as batch metrics
The streaming-specific concepts that this module introduced

blocks.title: 100 lessons. 54.7 hours. From working code to production-grade PySpark.
blocks.content: You started this track reading execution plans and end it building unified batch-streaming architectures. You can optimize joins, manage memory, evolve schemas, design fault-tolerant pipelines, test comprehensively, deploy safely, monitor continuously, and process data in both batch and near-real-time. The NordGrid scenario grew from 500K meters to 1.2M, from 4 regions to 12, from a single daily batch to a dual batch-streaming system. The senior-level track continues the journey: platform architecture for multi-team environments, advanced AQE and bucketing, schema registries, contract testing at scale, and the governance frameworks that make PySpark reliable at enterprise scale.
